sections.summary: This section defines 'market infrastructure' as licensed central counterparties, central securities depositories, clearing houses, exchanges, and trade repositories. It also redefines 'participant' to include authorized users, clearing members, nominees, and others who perform custody and settlement services under central securities depository rules.

sections.title: Definitions of financial sector terms and entities
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used throughout the Financial Sector Regulation Act, including 'supervised entity', 'statutory ombud', 'systemic event', and 'systemically important financial institution'. It specifies that certain terms have the same meaning as defined in other acts, such as the Income Tax Act and Trust Property Control Act. The definitions are critical for understanding the scope and application of the Act across various financial sector laws.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions establish foundational terms for data transfers involving financial institutions, particularly clarifying what constitutes a 'supervised entity' and 'systemically important financial institution' which may impact cross-border data flows and regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: A 'supervised entity' means each of the following: a licensed financial institution, a person with whom a licensed financial institution has entered into an outsourcing arrangement, and a representative of a financial institution.

A 'statutory ombud' means each of the following: The Adjudicator as defined in section 1(1) of the Pension Funds Act, the Adjudicator as defined in section 1(1) of the Friendly Societies Act, and other specified entities.
A 'systemic event' refers to events that could impact financial stability.
A 'systemically important financial institution' is defined as an institution that poses significant risk to the financial system.

sections.relevance_reason: Definitions of key terms for financial compliance

sections.title: Financial Sector Regulation Act alignment with other laws
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Financial Sector Regulation Act aligns with the Financial Sector Regulation Act. It requires that any provision of the Act that refers to a 'regulatory instrument' must be interpreted as a matter prescribed by the Authority where notice in the Gazette is specifically required. The section also specifies that references to inspections, prescribed fees, and reviews of decisions must be read as investigations, relevant fees determined under section 237, and reconsiderations by the Tribunal, respectively.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies how the Financial Sector Regulation Act interacts with other regulatory instruments and procedures, which is relevant for DTIAs when aligning data transfer mechanisms with existing regulatory frameworks.

requirements.text: A reference in this Act to an inspection must be read as a reference to an investigation in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
A reference in this Act to the Authority publishing information on a website must be read as a reference to publishing in the Register
The Authority may also publish information or documents on its website
A reference in this Act to a prescribed fee must be read as a reference to the relevant fee determined in terms of section 237 and Chapter 16 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
A reference in this Act to a review of a decision of the Authority must be read as a reference to a reconsideration of the decision by the Tribunal

sections.title: Licensing requirements for market infrastructure operators
sections.summary: This section mandates that persons operating as exchanges, central securities depositories, trade repositories, clearing houses, or central counterparties must be licensed under specified sections. Unlicensed entities cannot purport to be such entities or use names suggesting connections to licensed market infrastructure operators. The section defines prohibited activities for unlicensed persons to prevent market manipulation and ensure regulatory oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes licensing requirements for market infrastructure operators, which directly impacts cross-border data transfers by requiring licensed entities to handle financial data under specific regulatory frameworks. Unlicensed entities face restrictions on using names or descriptions that suggest market infrastructure connections.

requirements.text: A person who is not licensed as an exchange, central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house or central counterparty
A participant, authorised user, clearing member, approved nominee or issuer of listed securities

May not purport to be an exchange, central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house, central counterparty, external central counterparty, external trade repository, participant, authorised user, clearing member, approved nominee or issuer of listed securities
May not behave in a manner or use a name or description that suggests, signifies or implies connection to licensed market infrastructure operators

sections.source_quote: A person who is not— (a) licensed as an exchange, a central securities depository, a trade repository [or], a clearing house or a central counterparty; (b) a participant; (c) an authorised user; (d) a clearing member; (e) an approved nominee; [or] (f) an issuer of listed securities, may not purport to be an exchange, central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house, central counterparty, external central counterparty, external trade repository, participant, authorised user, clearing member, approved nominee or issuer of listed securities, as the case may be, or behave in a manner or use a name or description which suggests, signifies or implies that there is some connection between that person and an exchange, a central securities depository, trade repository, clearing house, central counterparty, external central counterparty, external trade repository, participant, authorised user or clearing member, as the case may be, where in fact no such connection exists.

sections.title: Exemptions for market infrastructure operators
sections.summary: This section outlines conditions for exemptions from licensing requirements for market infrastructure operators. Applicants must demonstrate that their operations do not conflict with public interest, frustrate the Act's objectives, or cause financial hardship to applicants or clients. The exemption process requires concurrence from the South African Reserve Bank and Prudential Authority, and includes specific criteria for external market infrastructure.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details conditions for exemptions from licensing requirements, which are critical for data transfer impact assessments involving market infrastructure operators. Exemptions require demonstrating no conflict with public interest, no frustration of Act objectives, and no financial hardship to applicants or clients.

The applicant must be based in an equivalent jurisdiction with authorization from the supervisory authority
The applicant must comply with any criteria prescribed in joint standards for exemption
The applicant must undertake to cooperate and share information with the Authority, South African Reserve Bank and Prudential Authority

sections.title: Co-operative financial institutions: regulatory authorization scope

sections.title: Governor may direct financial regulators to share information with Reserve Bank during crises
sections.summary: The Governor can direct financial sector regulators to provide specific information to the Reserve Bank for exercising powers under sections 14 or 15. During systemic events, regulators must assist the Reserve Bank by acting according to directives aimed at restructuring institutions, preventing risk spread, or increasing financial resilience. The Prudential Authority, Financial Sector Conduct Authority, and Financial Intelligence Centre must comply with such directives, with the National Credit Regulator requiring Minister consultation.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows the Governor to direct data sharing with the Reserve Bank during systemic events, requiring DTIAs for cross-border transfers of financial data to ensure compliance with crisis management protocols.

National Credit Regulator must comply with directives after Minister consultation on consumer credit matters

sections.title: Central securities depository operational requirements
sections.summary: This section amends section 29 by requiring that a central securities depository licence specifies the registered office and operational locations. It mandates that the depository may not operate at any other location without joint prior written approval from the Registrar Authority, Prudential Authority, and South African Reserve Bank. This ensures oversight of depository operations across multiple jurisdictions.

sections.heading: The Financial Stability Oversight Committee has the following functions:
sections.title: Financial Stability Oversight Committee functions for crisis management
sections.summary: The Financial Stability Oversight Committee serves as a forum for Reserve Bank and financial sector regulators to exchange views on financial stability activities. It makes recommendations to the Governor on systemically important financial institutions and advises the Minister and Reserve Bank on financial stability risks and crisis management. It also recommends steps for other state organs to assist in financial stability.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires DTIAs for cross-border data sharing when the Committee coordinates financial stability activities between regulators and the Reserve Bank.

sections.title: Designation of systemically important financial institutions
sections.summary: The Governor may designate financial institutions as systemically important financial institutions through written notice. This power cannot be delegated. Before designation, the Governor must notify the Financial Stability Oversight Committee and invite the institution to submit comments. The Governor must consider factors including size, complexity, interconnectedness, substitutes, Committee recommendations, and submissions when making designations. Designations may be revoked and must be published.

sections.heading: (1) To mitigate the risks that systemic events may occur, the Reserve Bank may,
sections.title: Prudential standards for systemically important institutions
sections.summary: The Reserve Bank may direct the Prudential Authority to impose prudential standards or regulator's directives on systemically important financial institutions to mitigate systemic risks. These standards may cover solvency, leverage ratios, liquidity, organizational structures, risk management, sectoral and geographical exposures, statistical returns, recovery planning, and other matters as prescribed by regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows for targeted prudential standards on systemically important institutions, which may impact cross-border data transfers for high-risk financial entities.

requirements.text: Reserve Bank may direct Prudential Authority to impose prudential standards on systemically important financial institutions

Prudential standards may cover solvency measures, capital requirements, leverage ratios, liquidity, organizational structures, risk management, sectoral and geographical exposures, statistical returns, recovery planning

sections.source_quote: 30. (1) To mitigate the risks that systemic events may occur, the Reserve Bank may, after consulting the Prudential Authority, direct the Prudential Authority to impose, either through prudential standards or regulator's directives, requirements applicable to one or more specific systemically important financial institutions or to such institutions generally in relation to any of the following matters: (a) Solvency measures and capital requirements, which may include requirements in relation to counter-cyclical capital buffers; (b) Leverage ratios; (c) Liquidity; (d) Organisational structures; (e) Risk management arrangements, including guarantee arrangements; (f) Sectoral and geographical exposures; (g) Required statistical returns; (h) Recovery and resolution planning; and (i) Any other matter in respect of which a prudential standard or regulator's directive may be made that is prescribed by Regulations made for this section on the recommendation of the Governor.

sections.title: Financial institution steps requiring Reserve Bank approval

sections.heading: The objective of the Prudential Authority is to—
sections.title: Objective of Prudential Authority
sections.summary: This section outlines the Prudential Authority's objectives, which include promoting financial institution safety and soundness, enhancing market infrastructure safety, protecting financial customers from institutional failure, and maintaining financial stability. These objectives guide the authority's regulatory activities.

For the segregation and portability of funds and securities held as collateral

In the case of a central counterparty, for the default procedures to be followed, including close-out procedures, in the event of a default of a clearing member

sections.title: Disclosure of conflicts of interest in Prudential Committee meetings
sections.summary: Prudential Committee members must disclose any interest in matters being considered, including related parties' interests. Disclosure must occur promptly after awareness. Members cannot participate in decisions affecting their disclosed interest unless they have disclosed it and other committee members determine the interest does not impair their functions. Non-compliance renders decisions void and requires reconsideration without the member.

sections.heading: (1) The Financial Sector Conduct Authority is hereby established, as a juristic
sections.title: Amendment of section 77 for insider definitions
sections.summary: This section amends section 77 to define 'inside information' as information that, if made public, would likely have a material effect on the price or value of securities listed on regulated markets or derivative instruments related to such securities. It also clarifies who qualifies as an 'insider' by including directors, employees, and shareholders of issuers of securities listed on regulated markets.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines inside information and insiders for regulatory purposes, impacting data transfer assessments by establishing criteria for insider information that may affect data handling and disclosure practices.

sections.source_quote: if it were made public, would be likely to have a material effect on the price or value of any security listed on a regulated market or of any derivative instrument related to such a security

sections.heading: The objective of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority is to—
sections.title: Amendment of section 78 for insider offenses
sections.summary: This section amends section 78 to define insider offenses, including the requirement that insiders who disclose inside information for the purpose of proper employment functions are not guilty if they prove on a balance of probabilities that disclosure was necessary for their employment functions and unrelated to securities trading. It also addresses offenses related to encouraging or discouraging others from trading based on inside information.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines insider offenses and their defenses, impacting data transfer assessments by establishing legal frameworks for insider information handling and disclosure.

sections.source_quote: An insider who knows that he or she has inside information and who encourages or causes another person to deal or discourages or stops another person from dealing in the securities listed on a regulated market or in derivative instruments related to such securities, to which the inside information relates or which are likely to be affected by it, commits an offence.

sections.heading: (1) A committee called the Executive Committee is hereby established for the
sections.title: Executive Committee structure and functions
sections.summary: The Executive Committee is established as a governing body for the Financial Sector Conduct Authority. It consists of the Commissioner and Deputy Commissioners and must oversee management, authorize memoranda of understanding, delegate powers to the Prudential Authority, adopt regulatory strategies, and manage subcommittees. The Committee has specific authority over regulatory instruments and licensing decisions.

sections.heading: (1) The Minister must, subject to due process, remove the Commissioner from
sections.title: Commissioner and Deputy Commissioner removal procedures
sections.summary: The Minister may remove the Commissioner from office if they become disqualified, with due process. The Commissioner must remove a Deputy Commissioner if they become disqualified, subject to Ministerial concurrence. Both may be suspended pending independent inquiry findings on performance, health, or misconduct. The Commissioner must submit removal reports to the National Assembly within 30 days.

sections.title: Executive Committee meeting procedures
sections.summary: The Executive Committee must hold meetings at times and places determined by the Commissioner, with a quorum of majority members. The Commissioner chairs meetings when present, otherwise a Deputy Commissioner chairs. The Commissioner may invite external representatives without voting rights. Minutes must be kept in a manner determined by the Commissioner.

sections.title: Post-inspection corrective actions
sections.summary: After a supervisory on-site visit or investigation, the Financial Sector Regulation Authority may direct respondents to take steps or refrain from actions to terminate or remedy irregularities disclosed. This includes powers to enforce compliance with the Financial Institutions (Protection of Funds) Act, but does not extend to orders under section 6D(2)(b) of that Act.

requirements.text: After a supervisory on-site visit or inspection or an investigation has been conducted under section 95
The [registrar] Authority may direct the respondent to take any steps or refrain from performing or continuing to perform any act
In order to terminate or remedy any irregularity or state of affairs disclosed by the supervisory on-site visit or investigation
Provided that the registrar may not make an order contemplated in section 6D(2)(b) of the Financial Institutions (Protection of Funds) Act

sections.title: Financial Sector Conduct Authority must follow regulatory strategy implementation
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Conduct Authority must implement the regulatory strategy adopted by the Executive Committee. This includes defining regulatory priorities, key outcomes, guiding principles for functions, matters to consider, administrative actions, transparency, consultation openness, and accountability. The strategy must align with section 58 and be reviewed annually with potential amendments.

sections.heading: (1) The Executive Committee may, in writing—
sections.title: Delegation of powers and duties by Financial Sector Conduct Authority
sections.summary: The Executive Committee may delegate powers and duties of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority to the Commissioner or Deputy Commissioner, except for specific powers. Delegations may be amended at any time. The Commissioner may delegate powers to Deputy Commissioners or staff members. Delegations must not constrain the Prudential Authority's objectives.

sections.title: Disclosure of interests by Executive Committee members

sections.title: Appeal mechanisms for financial decisions
sections.summary: This section amends section 105 to establish a grievance mechanism for persons aggrieved by decisions made by the Financial Sector Regulation Authority, exchanges, central securities depositories, or other entities under the Act. It covers appeals related to authorization, trading, and participation in securities markets.

sections.title: Amendment of fee determination authority in financial regulations
sections.summary: This section amends section 108 by substituting a subsection that allows the Authority to determine fees for matters under the Act. It specifies who must pay fees, the payment method, and interest for overdue fees. The Authority has discretion to set these fees within the framework of the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This amendment clarifies fee determination mechanisms but does not directly impact data transfer assessments as it pertains to fee structures rather than data flows.

Interest payable in respect of overdue fees is specified

sections.source_quote: The Authority may determine fees in respect of matters contemplated in this Act and, in relation to those fees, the person by whom the fee must be paid, the manner of payment thereof and, where necessary, the interest payable in respect of overdue fees.

sections.heading: (1) The financial sector regulators and the Reserve Bank must co-operate and
sections.title: Regulators and Reserve Bank cooperation
sections.summary: Financial sector regulators and the Reserve Bank must cooperate and collaborate on financial sector laws, the National Credit Act, and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act. This includes sharing information, adopting consistent regulatory strategies, coordinating actions, minimizing duplication, and reporting annually to the Minister and Cabinet.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates cooperation between regulators and the Reserve Bank on data handling, which impacts cross-border data transfers through shared regulatory frameworks and information flows.

generally assist and support each other in pursuing their objectives in terms of financial sector laws, the National Credit Act and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act
inform each other about, and share information about, matters of common interest

The financial sector regulators and the Reserve Bank must, at least annually as part of their annual reports, or on request, report to the Minister, the Cabinet member responsible for administering the National Credit Act and the National Assembly on measures taken to co-operate and collaborate with each other

sections.source_quote: 76. (1) The financial sector regulators and the Reserve Bank must co-operate and collaborate when performing their functions in terms of financial sector laws, the National Credit Act, and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act, and must for this purpose— (a) generally assist and support each other in pursuing their objectives in terms of financial sector laws, the National Credit Act and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act; (b) inform each other about, and share information about, matters of common interest; (c) strive to adopt consistent regulatory strategies, including addressing regulatory and supervisory challenges; (d) co-ordinate, to the extent appropriate, actions in terms of financial sector laws, the National Credit Act and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act, including in relation to— (i) standards and other regulatory instruments, including similar instruments provided for in terms of the National Credit Act and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act; (ii) licensing; (iii) supervisory on-site inspections and investigations; (iv) actions to enforce financial sector laws, the National Credit Act and the Financial Intelligence Centre Act; (v) information sharing; (vi) recovery and resolution; and (vii) reporting by financial institutions, including statutory reporting and data collection measures; (e) minimise the duplication of effort and expense, including by establishing and using, where appropriate, common or shared databases and other facilities; (f) agree on attendance at relevant international forums; and (g) develop, to the extent that is appropriate, consistent policy positions, including for the purpose of presentation and negotiation at relevant South African and international forums.

sections.title: Clearing house licensing requirements for central counterparties
sections.summary: This section amends section 110 by deleting subsection (5) and adding a new subsection (6). It requires clearing houses performing central counterparty functions to be licensed by the South African Reserve Bank and Prudential Authority by January 2022. sections.title: False information in registration applications

sections.title: Clearing house licensing requirements for central counterparties
sections.summary: This section amends section 110 by deleting subsection (5) and adding a new subsection (6). It requires clearing houses performing central counterparty functions to be licensed by the South African Reserve Bank and Prudential Authority by January 2022. sections.summary: When an administrative decision or step by the Financial Sector Conduct Authority or Agency affects another person's rights, it must comply with the Promotion of Administrative Justice Act, 2000, unless a different fair administrative procedure is specified in this Act or the Financial Sector Regulation Act.

sections.heading: (1) The Financial Sector Inter-Ministerial Council is hereby established.
sections.title: Establishment of Financial Sector Inter-Ministerial Council
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Inter-Ministerial Council is established to facilitate cooperation between Cabinet members responsible for financial sector regulation. Its members include the Minister and specific Cabinet members for consumer protection, health, and economic development. The Council's objective is to provide a forum for discussing matters of common interest among financial regulators.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a governance body for financial regulators but does not directly affect data transfers or cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: The Financial Sector Inter-Ministerial Council is hereby established.

The objective of the Inter-Ministerial Council is to facilitate co-operation and collaboration between Cabinet members responsible for administering legislation relevant to the regulation and supervision of the financial sector by providing a forum for discussion and consideration of matters of common interest.

The members of the Inter-Ministerial Council are—(a) the Minister; (b) the Cabinet members responsible for consumer protection and consumer credit matters; (c) the Cabinet member responsible for health; and (d) the Cabinet member responsible for economic development.

sections.source_quote: 83. (1) The Financial Sector Inter-Ministerial Council is hereby established. (3) The members of the Inter-Ministerial Council are—(a) the Minister; (b) the Cabinet members responsible for consumer protection and consumer credit matters; (c) the Cabinet member responsible for health; and (d) the Cabinet member responsible for economic development.

sections.summary: This section grants the Authority the power to establish conduct standards or regulator's directives for monitoring and identifying possible contraventions of the Financial Sector Regulation Act. It also allows the Authority to create conduct standards for the disclosure of inside information. The Authority must consult with relevant regulated markets before implementing these standards.

sections.title: Directorate of Market Abuse established under Financial Sector Regulation Act
sections.summary: This section establishes the Directorate of Market Abuse as the continuation of the Insider Trading Directorate from previous legislation. It specifies the functions, powers, and duties of the Directorate, including conducting investigations into offences under sections 78, 80, and 81 of the Act and section 135(2) of the Financial Sector Regulation Act. The Directorate consists of members and alternate members appointed by the Authority.

sections.heading: (1) (a) The Inter-Ministerial Council must, as soon as practicable following the
sections.title: Independent evaluation of financial sector cooperation
sections.summary: The Inter-Ministerial Council must commission independent evaluations of cooperative mechanisms between financial regulators, the Reserve Bank, Financial Intelligence Centre, Council for Medical Schemes, and Competition Commission. Evaluations must assess memoranda of understanding, consultations, and compliance with relevant sections. The Council may also evaluate at the request of a financial sector regulator and must table evaluations in Parliament with a report.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates independent evaluations of financial sector cooperation but does not directly impact data transfers or cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: The Inter-Ministerial Council must, as soon as practicable following the expiration of the six month period described in section 77(1), commission an independent evaluation of the establishment of co-operative and collaborative mechanisms between the financial sector regulators, the Reserve Bank, the Financial Intelligence Centre, the Council for Medical Schemes and the Competition Commission.

The Inter-Ministerial Council must, every two years after the initial independent evaluation referred to in paragraph (a), commission an independent evaluation of the effectiveness of co-operative and collaborative mechanisms between the financial sector regulators, the Reserve Bank, the Financial Intelligence Centre, the Council for Medical Schemes and the Competition Commission.

An evaluation in terms of this section must at least contain an analysis and evaluation of the memoranda of understanding required in terms of section 77, the outcome of any and all consultations in terms of section 78, and compliance with those sections.

The Inter-Ministerial Council may on its own initiative, or at the request of a financial sector regulator, at any time commission an independent evaluation of the effectiveness of co-operation and collaboration between the financial sector regulators, the Reserve Bank, the Financial Intelligence Centre, the Council for Medical Schemes and the Competition Commission.

When a financial sector regulator makes a request for an evaluation, the Inter-Ministerial Council must consider the request and the concerns raised in the request regarding the effectiveness of co-operation and collaboration, and, if the Council rejects the request, provide the financial sector regulator that made the request with the reasons for rejecting the request.

Any evaluation commissioned by the Inter-Ministerial Council in terms of this section must be tabled in Parliament immediately following the Council's consideration of the evaluation, and must be accompanied by a report from the Council on the evaluation's contents.

sections.source_quote: 86. (1) (a) The Inter-Ministerial Council must, as soon as practicable following the expiration of the six month period described in section 77(1), commission an independent evaluation of the establishment of co-operative and collaborative mechanisms between the financial sector regulators, the Reserve Bank, the Financial Intelligence Centre, the Council for Medical Schemes and the Competition Commission. (b) The Inter-Ministerial Council must, every two years after the initial independent evaluation referred to in paragraph (a), commission an independent evaluation of the effectiveness of co-operative and collaborative mechanisms between the financial sector regulators, the Reserve Bank, the Financial Intelligence Centre, the Council for Medical Schemes and the Competition Commission.

sections.heading: (1) A financial sector regulator may establish an administrative action committee
sections.title: Administrative action committee composition requirements
sections.summary: A financial sector regulator can create an administrative action committee to review matters referred by itself. Committee members must include a retired judge or an attorney with at least 10 years legal experience. Additional members may include non-Prudential Committee or Executive Committee members. The chairperson must be appointed from this group. Disqualified persons cannot join the committee.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines committee structure for regulatory oversight, relevant for ensuring independent governance in cross-border financial regulatory actions.

Disqualified persons may not be appointed to or remain members of the committee

sections.title: Authority sharing financial information with regulators
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Regulation Authority may share information with specified entities including the Takeover Regulation Panel, South African Reserve Bank, Prudential Authority, Financial Intelligence Centre, National Treasury, and persons responsible for insider trading regulation. This applies to information related to matters dealt with under this Chapter. The sharing is permitted without specific conditions beyond the scope of the recipient entities.

sections.title: Administrative action committee meeting procedures
sections.summary: Committee meetings are convened by the chairperson and chaired by the chairperson or a designated member. The committee determines its own procedures subject to regulator directions. The regulator must maintain written minutes of all meetings in a manner specified by itself.

sections.title: Investigation powers for financial misconduct
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Regulation Authority may investigate complaints or allegations of misconduct by securities service providers. If the Authority has reason to believe a contravention is occurring, it may direct the respondent to provide information, documents, or records reasonably required about their services. The Authority may also require the respondent to appear before it at a specified time and place.

sections.title: Revocation of financial decisions
sections.summary: Financial sector regulators can revoke decisions if they were made due to fraud, inaccurate information, or invalidity. Revocation must be done after notifying the affected person and giving them at least 14 days to submit comments. Regulators cannot revoke decisions that would harm existing rights or if legal proceedings are ongoing.

sections.heading: (1) A regulatory instrument must not be made unless the maker—
sections.title: Pre-approval requirements for regulatory instruments
sections.summary: A regulatory instrument cannot be enacted without the maker first publishing a draft, explaining the need and intended operation, detailing expected impacts, and inviting submissions. Submissions must be received within six weeks, and the maker must submit the final instrument to Parliament. Financial sector regulators must share documents with other regulators and relevant bodies.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires pre-submission consultation for regulatory instruments, ensuring transparency and stakeholder input before implementation.

has submitted the regulatory instrument to Parliament in terms of section 103(1) after considering submissions
provide a copy of the documents to the other financial sector regulator, the Reserve Bank, the National Credit Regulator, the Council for Medical Schemes and the Director-General
provide a copy of the documents to market infrastructure licensing providers if the instrument affects securities services

sections.source_quote: 98. (1) A regulatory instrument must not be made unless the maker— (a) has published— (i) a draft of the regulatory instrument; (ii) a statement explaining the need for and the intended operation of the regulatory instrument; (iii) a statement of the expected impact of the regulatory instrument; and (iv) a notice inviting submissions in relation to the regulatory instrument and stating where, how and by when submissions are to be made; and (b) has, once submissions referred to in paragraph (a)(iv) have been received and considered, submitted the regulatory instrument to Parliament in terms of section 103(1). (2) The period allowed for making submissions referred to in subsection (1)(a)(iv) must be at least six weeks.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory process for financial instruments

sections.title: Repeating consultation process for materially different instruments
sections.summary: If a maker intends to create a regulatory instrument in a materially different form than the published draft, they must repeat the entire consultation process outlined in section 98 before finalizing the instrument.

sections.title: Urgent regulatory instruments with expedited consultation

sections.source_quote: 100. (1) If the maker of a regulatory instrument determines that compliance with section 98 or 99 is likely to lead to prejudice to financial customers or harm to the financial system, or defeat the object of the proposed regulatory instrument, the maker must before making the instrument— (a) publish— (i) a draft of the regulatory instrument and a statement explaining the need for and the intended operation of the regulatory instrument; (ii) a notice inviting submissions in relation to the regulatory instrument and stating where, how and by when submissions are to be made; and (iii) a statement of the reasons why the delay involved in complying with sections 98 and 99 is considered likely to lead to prejudice to financial customers or harm to the financial system, or defeat the object of the proposed regulatory instrument; and (b) submit the regulatory instrument to Parliament in terms of section 103(2). (2) The period allowed for making submissions in terms of subsection (1)(a)(ii) must be at least seven days.

sections.title: Regulatory instrument publication and implementation
sections.summary: The maker must consider all submissions received by the consultation deadline and parliamentary deliberations when deciding whether to enact a regulatory instrument. Once made, the instrument is published in the Register and takes effect immediately or on a later date specified in the instrument.

(2) A regulatory instrument must be published in the Register after it is made. (3) A regulatory instrument comes into effect— (a) on the date the instrument is published in the Register; or (b) if the instrument provides that it comes into effect on a later date, on the later date.

sections.title: Prudential standards for financial institutions
sections.summary: The Prudential Authority may establish prudential standards for financial institutions providing financial products or securities services, market infrastructures, and key persons. These standards must aim at ensuring safety and soundness, reducing financial crime risks, and maintaining financial stability. Standards may cover capital adequacy, liquidity, asset quality, and matters regulated by specific financial sector laws.

sections.heading: The Prudential Authority and the Financial Sector Conduct Authority may make
sections.title: Joint standards-making authority for financial regulators
sections.summary: The Prudential Authority and Financial Sector Conduct Authority have joint power to create standards on matters where either regulator has authority to make standards. These standards can cover additional matters specified in the section, including fit and proper person requirements, governance structures, financial management, and risk management. The standards must be made to achieve the objectives outlined in sections 33 and 57 of the Act.

sections.heading: (1) To achieve the respective objectives of the financial sector regulators as set
sections.title: Regulatory standards for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial sector regulators may establish standards for financial institutions to achieve objectives in sections 33 and 57 of the Act. These standards cover fit and proper person requirements, governance structures, financial management, risk management, outsourcing, record-keeping, and reporting. Standards must be published on the regulator's website and may be amended or revoked by the same regulator. The standards require concurrence from the Reserve Bank for payment services and financial stability measures.

sections.heading: (1) The Financial Sector Conduct Authority may not make a standard that
sections.title: Reserve Bank concurrence for standards
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Conduct Authority cannot establish standards for payment services without Reserve Bank approval. Financial sector regulators also cannot create standards aimed at maintaining financial stability without Reserve Bank concurrence. These provisions ensure that critical financial infrastructure standards require coordination between regulators and the central bank.

sections.heading: (1) A person may not provide, as a business or part of a business, a financial
sections.title: Licensing requirements for financial activities
sections.summary: A person cannot provide financial products, services, or market infrastructure without a license under specific financial sector laws, the National Credit Act, or the National Payment System Act. This includes financial products designated in section 2 or services in section 3. Licensing applies to contractors only if a responsible authority requires it in a standard. A person cannot describe themselves as licensed unless they have a valid license, and a suspended or revoked license means they are not licensed.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes licensing requirements that could impact data transfer by restricting who can handle financial data without proper authorization.

sections.title: Conditions for financial sector license applications
sections.summary: This section outlines the conditions that must be met for a financial institution to be considered for a license. It specifies that the applicant must have sufficient financial resources and capacity to comply with financial sector laws, and that issuing the license must not be contrary to the interests of financial customers, the financial sector, or the public interest. The responsible authority must determine the application within three months, with possible extensions up to nine months total.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes timeframes and conditions for license determination, relevant to DTIAs as it affects the timeline and scope of regulatory oversight for financial institutions.

sections.heading: The matters to be taken into account in relation to an application for a licence
sections.title: Matters considered in license applications
sections.summary: This section lists the factors that the responsible authority must consider when evaluating an application for a financial license. These include the responsible authority's objectives, the applicant's financial and other resources, fit and proper person requirements, governance and risk management arrangements, and whether the application contains false or misleading statements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it defines the criteria used to assess license applications, impacting the evaluation of financial institutions' compliance with regulatory requirements.

sections.source_quote: 115. The matters to be taken into account in relation to an application for a licence include— (a) the objective of the responsible authority as set out in section 33 or 57; (b) the financial and other resources of and available to the applicant; (c) fit and proper person requirements applicable to the applicant and to any key person or significant owner of the applicant; (d) the governance and risk management arrangements of the applicant; and (e) whether the applicant made a statement that is false or misleading, including by omission, in or in relation to the application.

sections.heading: (1) A licensee must promptly report any of the following to the responsible
sections.title: Reporting contraventions to responsible authority
sections.summary: This section requires licensees to promptly report to the responsible authority any contraventions of financial sector laws, regulatory directives, enforceable undertakings, court orders, or tribunal decisions that are material. It also mandates reporting if the licensee becomes aware that information provided in the license application was false or misleading. The reported information is not admissible in criminal proceedings except for perjury cases.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it specifies the reporting obligations for contraventions, which can impact data transfer and processing activities across borders.

sections.title: License variations by responsible authority
sections.summary: This section allows the responsible authority to vary a license by notice to the licensee if doing so will assist in achieving the authority's objectives as set out in section 33 or 57. A license variation may include removing, varying, or adding conditions to the license, or changing the categories of financial products, services, or customers the license relates to. The variation takes effect on the date of the notice or a later date specified in the notice.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it outlines the conditions under which licenses can be varied, impacting the scope of data processing and transfer activities.

requirements.text: The responsible authority may vary the license if doing so will assist in achieving the objective of the responsible authority as set out in section 33 or 57
A variation of a license may include removing or varying a condition of the license, or adding a condition
A variation of a license may include changing the categories of financial products, financial services, or financial customers to which the license relates
A variation of a license takes effect on the date of the notice or a later date specified in the notice

sections.heading: (1) The responsible authority that issued a licence may, by notice to the licensee,
sections.title: Conditions for suspending a financial license
sections.summary: The responsible authority may suspend a financial license for up to 30 days if the licensee applies for suspension, contravenes a material condition of the license, fails to pay license fees for 30+ days, or violates foreign laws corresponding to financial sector laws. Suspension is prohibited if it harms financial customers or undermines financial sector laws. The authority may later revoke the suspension.
sections.dtia_summary: Suspension conditions require material contraventions of financial laws, license conditions, or payment obligations. This section impacts DTIA by defining when suspension is permissible and the conditions that must be met to avoid disrupting financial services.

A condition of the license has been contravened or not complied with in a material way
The licensee has contravened in a material way a financial sector law
The licensee has contravened in a material way a prudential standard, conduct standard, or joint standard
The licensee has contravened in a material way a regulator's directive or directive under section 202
The licensee has contravened in a material way an enforceable undertaking
The licensee has contravened in a material way a court order under financial sector law
The licensee has contravened in a material way a tribunal decision
The licensee has contravened in a material way a law of a foreign country corresponding to a financial sector law
Information provided in or in relation to an application was false or misleading in a material way
Suspension is necessary to prevent serious contravention of financial sector law
Suspension is necessary to prevent financial customers suffering material prejudice
Fees for the license, levies, or administrative penalties are unpaid for at least 30 days
The suspension would not be in the best interests of financial customers
The suspension would frustrate the objects of a financial sector law applicable to the license
The responsible authority may revoke the suspension at any time

sections.title: Conditions for revoking a financial license
sections.summary: The responsible authority may revoke a financial license if the licensee applies for revocation, contravenes material conditions of the license, or ceases conducting licensed business. Revocation is prohibited if it harms financial customers or undermines financial sector laws. Revocation takes effect immediately or on a later date specified in the notice.
sections.dtia_summary: Revocation conditions include material contraventions of license terms and business cessation. This section impacts DTIA by defining when revocation is permissible and the timing of its effect.

sections.heading: (1) The responsible authority that suspended or revoked a licence may, by notice
sections.title: Conditions for resuming licensed activity after suspension or revocation
sections.summary: The responsible authority may allow a licensee to continue licensed activities during suspension or revocation if conditions specified in the license are met. These conditions must ensure fair treatment of financial customers or facilitate orderly termination of licensed activities. Compliance with such notices does not constitute a contravention of sections 111 or 162.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows resumption of activities after suspension/revocation with conditions to ensure customer fairness and orderly termination. This section impacts DTIA by defining permissible conditions for resuming activities.

sections.title: Notice and submissions before varying, suspending or revoking a license
sections.summary: Before varying, suspending, or revoking a license, the responsible authority must notify the licensee of the proposed action and reasons, invite submissions within a reasonable period (at least one month), and consider all submissions made during this period. The authority may take action without full compliance if it would prejudice financial customers, financial stability, or the object of the action. The authority must provide written reasons if it takes action without full compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires notice and submissions before license actions, with exceptions for urgent cases. This section impacts DTIA by defining procedural safeguards for license actions.

sections.heading: (1) The responsible authority may, in writing, determine procedures and
sections.title: Procedures for license applications
sections.summary: The responsible authority may determine written procedures and requirements for license applications, including institutional form, business activities, financial capacity, fit and proper persons, and operational governance arrangements. Applications must follow these procedures, and the authority must publish the requirements.

sections.heading: This Part applies in relation to licences in terms of all financial sector laws.
sections.title: Licensing concurrence requirements for financial sector regulators
sections.summary: This section mandates that financial sector regulators must concurrence on licensing matters before taking any action. The concurrence requirement applies to all financial sector regulators when issuing, varying, suspending, revoking licenses, or granting exemptions under financial sector laws. The section establishes that such concurrence is a prerequisite for regulatory actions affecting financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires concurrence from multiple financial regulators before licensing actions, impacting cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions by necessitating coordination between regulators.

requirements.text: Financial sector regulators must concurrence on licensing matters

sections.heading: (1) The responsible authority may not take any of the actions specified in
sections.title: Regulatory concurrence for financial licensing actions
sections.summary: This section prohibits financial sector regulators from taking specific licensing actions without concurrence from other regulators. Issuing, varying, suspending, revoking licenses, or granting exemptions under financial sector laws require concurrence from other financial sector regulators. If the action affects a systemically important financial institution, the Reserve Bank must also concurrence. This creates a multi-regulator approval process for critical financial actions.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates concurrence from multiple regulators for licensing actions involving systemically important financial institutions, which impacts data transfer assessments by requiring coordination across regulatory bodies.

requirements.text: The responsible authority may not take any action specified in subsection (2) without concurrence from other financial sector regulators

sections.source_quote: 126. (1) The responsible authority may not take any of the actions specified in subsection (2) unless— (a) the other financial sector regulator has concurred; and (b) if the action relates to or affects a systemically important financial institution, the Reserve Bank has also concurred.

sections.title: Mandatory disclosure of licenses
sections.summary: This section requires financial institutions to disclose licenses to relevant authorities. The disclosure must include specific information about the license and the financial institution. The section mandates that financial institutions provide detailed information to the responsible authority when required, ensuring transparency in the licensing process. This disclosure mechanism supports regulatory oversight of financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires disclosure of licenses to regulators, which impacts data transfer assessments by ensuring transparency in financial institution licensing and data handling practices.

sections.title: Regulatory information gathering powers
sections.summary: This section grants financial sector regulators the authority to gather information through on-site inspections and investigations. Regulators can request specified information or documents from supervised entities to assist in performing their functions under financial sector laws. The Council for Medical Schemes may also exercise these powers in relation to medical schemes, with specific adaptations to the regulatory framework. This enables regulators to monitor compliance with financial sector laws.

sections.title: Regulatory information requests for compliance
sections.summary: This section requires financial institutions to comply with written notices from regulators requesting specific information or documents relevant to regulatory functions. Supervised entities must provide the requested information or documents within the notice's requirements. Regulators may also require verification of information by an auditor approved by the responsible authority. This ensures that financial institutions can demonstrate compliance with financial sector laws through documented evidence.

sections.title: Investigator entry and search powers with consent or warrant
sections.summary: An investigator may enter premises for an investigation with prior consent from the person in control of the premises after informing them of the purpose and scope of entry and search, or without consent if authorized by a warrant. The entry also permits subsequent search of premises for evidence of financial law contraventions or as requested under section 135(1)(b). Investigators must conduct searches with strict regard to constitutional rights, decency, and good order.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines conditions for lawful entry and search of premises by investigators, including consent requirements, warrant authorizations, and procedural safeguards that impact data transfer assessments when financial institutions are subject to cross-border investigations.

sections.source_quote: 137. (1) An investigator may, for the purposes of conducting an investigation, do any of the following: (a) Enter any premises— (i) with the prior consent of— (aa) in the case of a private residence, the person apparently in control of the business reasonably believed to be conducted at the private residence, and the occupant of the private residence or the part of the private residence to be entered; or (bb) in the case of any other premises, the person apparently in control of the premises, after informing that person that— (AA) granting consent will enable the investigator to enter the premises and for the investigator to subsequently search the premises as referred to in paragraph (b) or (c), and to do anything contemplated in subsection (6); and (BB) he or she is under no obligation to admit the investigator in the absence of a warrant; or (ii) without prior consent and without prior notice to any person— (aa) if the entry is authorised by a warrant; or (bb) with the prior authority of the head of a financial sector regulator or a senior staff member of the financial sector regulator delegated to perform the function, if the head of a financial sector regulator or senior staff member on reasonable grounds believes that— (AA) a warrant will be issued under section 138(1) if applied for; (BB) the delay in obtaining the warrant is likely to defeat the purpose for which entry of the premises is sought; and (CC) it is necessary to enter the premises to conduct the investigation and search the premises as referred to in paragraph (b) or (c), and to do anything contemplated in subsection (6); (b) if the investigation is one referred to in section 135(1)(a), search the premises for evidence of a contravention of a financial sector law; or (c) if the investigation is one referred to in section 135(1)(b), search the premises pursuant to the request, subject to section 251.

sections.title: Warrant requirements for investigator entry
sections.summary: Warrants must be issued by a judge or magistrate for investigator entry into premises. The warrant must specify the grounds for entry, including whether the investigation relates to section 135(1)(a) or (1)(b), and must be signed by the issuing judge or magistrate. Investigators must display the warrant on premises if no one is present when executed.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the legal framework for warrants authorizing investigator entry, which impacts data transfer assessments by defining when and how cross-border data access can occur during financial investigations.

sections.source_quote: 138. (1) (a) Moatlhodi kgotsa magiseterata yo o nang le taolo o ka rebola thebolo mabapi le maitlhomo a Karolo eno mo kopong ya mmatlisisi. (b) Moatlhodi kgotsa magiseterata o ka rebola thebolo go ya ka karolo eno— (i) ka kopo e e kwetsweng ke mmatlisisi e e tlhalosang ka fa tlase ga maikano kgotsa netefatso gore goreng go tlhokega go tsena le go batlisisa kwa mafelong; le (ii) fa go tswa mo tshedimosetsong e e ikanetsweng kgotsa netefaditsweng magiseterata kgotsa moatlhodi a lemoga gore— (aa) mo lebakeng la patlisiso e e ka fa tlase ga karolo 135(1)(a), go— (AA) na le mabaka a a utlwalang a go belaela gore molao wa lephata la ditshelete o tlotswe, o ka tswa o tlolwa kgotsa o ka tlolwa; (BB) entry and investigation of the premises are likely to yield information pertaining to the contravention; and (CC) entry and investigation of those premises is reasonably necessary for the purposes of the investigation; (bb) in the case of an investigation under section 135(1)(b), that there are reasonable grounds to believe that the investigation is necessary to comply with a request referred to in that section.

sections.title: Right to object to incriminating information during investigations
sections.summary: Individuals may object to answering questions or producing documents during financial investigations if the response could incriminate them. Incriminating answers and documents are not admissible in criminal proceedings except for perjury or specific contraventions under section 273.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides protections for individuals during financial investigations, which can impact data transfer assessments when financial institutions are subject to cross-border data collection procedures.

requirements.text: Individuals may object to answering questions or producing documents if response could incriminate them
Incriminating answers and documents are not admissible in criminal proceedings except for perjury or section 273 contraventions

sections.source_quote: 140. (1) (a) A person who is questioned, or required to produce a document or information, during a supervisory on-site inspection contemplated in section 132, or by an investigator in terms of Part 4 of this Chapter, whether in response to a notice contemplated in section 136, or when an investigator is exercising the powers contemplated in section 137(6)(a)(iii) to (v), may object to answering the question or to producing the document or the information on the grounds that the answer, the contents of the document or the information may tend to incriminate the person. (b) On such an objection, the official of the financial sector regulator conducting the supervisory on-site inspection or the investigator may require the question to be answered or the document or information to be produced, in which case the person must answer the question or produce the document. (c) An incriminating answer given, and an incriminating document or information produced, as required in terms of paragraph (b), is not admissible in evidence against the person in any criminal proceedings, except in criminal proceedings for perjury or in which that person is tried for a contravention of section 273 based on the false or misleading nature of the answer.

sections.heading: (1) A person may give a written undertaking to the responsible authority
sections.title: Enforceable undertakings for financial sector compliance
sections.summary: The responsible authority may accept enforceable undertakings from financial institutions to comply with financial sector laws. If an institution breaches the undertaking, the authority may suspend its license. The authority must publish all undertakings, variations, and withdrawals. The Tribunal can issue orders to enforce compliance, including remedial actions for past contraventions and future conduct. Orders can be enforced as civil judgments if not complied with.

sections.heading: (1) Subject to subsections (3) and (4), a person is a significant owner of a
sections.title: Definition of significant owner of financial institution
sections.summary: A person is a significant owner of a financial institution if they, directly or indirectly, alone or together with related or inter-related persons, have the ability to control or influence materially the business or strategy of the institution. This ability can be demonstrated by holding 15% voting power, requiring consent for 15% voting power appointments, or holding a qualifying stake in the institution.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines who qualifies as a significant owner for DTIA purposes, impacting how ownership structures are assessed for cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions.

requirements.text: A person is a significant owner of a financial institution if the person, directly or indirectly, alone or together with a related or inter-related person, has the ability to control or influence materially the business or strategy of the financial institution.

The person has the ability referred to in subsection (1) if the person, directly or indirectly, alone or together with related or inter-related person, has the power to appoint 15% of the members of the governing body of the financial institution.

The person has the ability referred to in subsection (1) if the consent of the person, alone or together with related or inter-related person, is required for the appointment of 15% of the members of a governing body of the financial institution.

The person has the ability referred to in subsection (1) if the person, directly or indirectly, alone or together with related or inter-related person, holds a qualifying stake in the financial institution.

sections.heading: (1) For the purposes of this section, a financial institution refers only to—
sections.title: Regulatory requirements for significant owners
sections.summary: Significant owners must obtain prior written approval from the responsible authority for financial sector law before effecting arrangements that change their ownership or influence over a financial institution. This includes arrangements that increase or decrease their ability to control or influence the institution's business or strategy. The authority must also ensure that such arrangements do not prejudicially affect the financial institution's prudent management or financial soundness.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets requirements for significant owners to ensure regulatory compliance before ownership changes, which impacts data transfer assessments involving financial institutions.

requirements.text: A person may not effect any arrangement that will result in the person, alone or together with related or inter-related person, increasing or decreasing the extent of the ability to control or influence materially the business or strategy of the financial institution without prior written approval from the responsible authority for financial sector law.
A person may not effect any arrangement that will result in the person ceasing to be a significant owner without prior notification to the responsible authority for financial sector law.

The responsible authority must be satisfied that the person becoming a significant owner or any arrangement will not prejudicially affect the prudent management and financial soundness of the financial institution.
The responsible authority must be satisfied that the person meets and is reasonably likely to continue to meet applicable fit and proper person requirements.

sections.summary: Financial sector regulators must establish standards for significant owners regarding personal character qualities, competence, financial standing, and the definition of increases or decreases in their ability to control or influence a financial institution. These standards must be complied with by significant owners to ensure they meet fit and proper requirements.

sections.heading: (1) The Prudential Authority may designate members of a group of companies as
sections.title: Designation of financial conglomerates by Prudential Authority
sections.summary: The Prudential Authority may designate members of a group of companies as a financial conglomerate, which must include an eligible financial institution and its holding company. Designation must be for prudential supervision purposes and requires prior notice and submissions from the holding company. The Authority must consult the Financial Sector Conduct Authority and consider risks to supervision, submissions, and regulatory prescriptions. Designation may be granted without complying with subsection (3) if it prevents material prejudice to financial customers or financial stability.
sections.dtia_summary: Designation of financial conglomerates requires risk assessment of group structures, holding company submissions, and consultation with the Financial Sector Conduct Authority. This affects cross-border data flows within conglomerates by mandating specific governance structures and risk management.

sections.heading: (1) The power of the Prudential Authority to make prudential standards extends
sections.title: Prudential standards for financial conglomerate holding companies

These standards can cover financial exposures within conglomerates, governance structures, reporting requirements for non-financial institutions, and risk management related to the conglomerate's financial stability. The Financial Sector Conduct Authority also has similar powers regarding conduct standards.

The Financial Sector Conduct Authority also has similar powers regarding compliance with financial laws.

sections.heading: (1) The responsible authority for a financial sector law may, by order served on
sections.title: Administrative penalties for financial sector violations
sections.summary: This section empowers responsible authorities to impose administrative penalties on persons who contravene financial sector laws or enforceable undertakings. Penalties must consider deterrence, cooperation with regulators, severity of contravention, financial impact, previous violations, and effects on financial stability. Penalties cannot be imposed if prosecution has already commenced, and must be published.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes penalty mechanisms that impact data transfer assessments by requiring regulators to consider financial stability implications when imposing penalties for data-related violations.

sections.heading: (1) The responsible authority that makes an administrative penalty order may file
sections.title: Filing administrative penalty orders with court
sections.summary: The responsible authority that issues administrative penalty orders may file a certified copy of the order with a competent court if the penalty amount remains unpaid and no reconsideration or judicial review applications have been made within the prescribed timeframe or if such applications have been finally disposed of. The filed order functions as a civil judgment and can be enforced as if legally issued in that court.

requirements.text: The responsible authority may file a certified copy of an administrative penalty order with a competent court
The order must be filed if the penalty amount has not been paid as required
The order must be filed if no reconsideration or judicial review applications have been lodged by the end of the application period
The order must be filed if reconsideration or judicial review applications have been finally disposed of
The filed order has the effect of a civil judgment and may be enforced as if lawfully given in that court

sections.source_quote: 170. (1) The responsible authority that makes an administrative penalty order may file with the registrar of a competent court a certified copy of the order if— (a) the amount payable in terms of the order has not been paid as required by the order; and (b) either— (i) no application for reconsideration of the order in terms of a financial sector law, or for judicial review in terms of the Promotion of Administrative Justice Act of the Tribunal's decision, has been lodged by the end of the period for making such applications; or (ii) if such an application has been made, proceedings on the application have been finally disposed of. (2) The order, on being filed, has the effect of a civil judgment, and may be enforced as if lawfully given in that court.
sections.relevance_reason: penalty enforcement, court procedures irrelevant to data compliance

sections.heading: All amounts recovered by a responsible authority as administrative penalties
sections.title: Application of administrative penalty funds
sections.summary: All administrative penalties collected by a responsible authority must first reimburse the authority for its reasonable and proper costs and expenses related to enforcing the penalty order. Any remaining funds after these reimbursements must then be paid into the National Revenue Fund.

requirements.text: All administrative penalties must be applied first to reimburse the responsible authority for its costs and expenses
The balance after reimbursement must be paid into the National Revenue Fund

sections.source_quote: 171. All amounts recovered by a responsible authority as administrative penalties must be applied— (a) first, to reimburse the responsible authority for its costs and expenses reasonably and properly incurred in connection with the relevant contraven- tion, making the order and enforcing it; and (b) then, the balance after applying the amount in accordance with paragraph (a) must be paid into the National Revenue Fund.

sections.heading: When determining the sentence to impose on a person convicted of an offence in
sections.title: Administrative penalties considered in sentencing
sections.summary: When determining a sentence for a person convicted of a financial sector law offence, a court must take into account any administrative penalty order made for the same set of facts. This ensures penalties are factored into judicial outcomes.

sections.heading: (1) The Ombud Council is hereby established.
sections.title: Establishment of the Ombud Council
sections.summary: The Ombud Council is established as a juristic person and a national public entity under the Public Finance Management Act. Its Chairperson serves as the accounting authority for the Council under that Act.

requirements.text: The Ombud Council is hereby established
The Ombud Council is a juristic person
The Ombud Council is a national public entity for the purposes of the Public Finance Management Act
The Chairperson of the Ombud Council is the accounting authority of the Ombud Council for the purposes of the Public Finance Management Act

sections.source_quote: 175. (1) The Ombud Council is hereby established. (2) The Ombud Council is a juristic person. (3) The Ombud Council is a national public entity for the purposes of the Public Finance Management Act, and notwithstanding section 49(2) of the Public Finance Management Act, the Chairperson of the Ombud Council is the accounting authority of the Ombud Council for the purposes of that Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure amendment mechanics and public finance

sections.heading: The objective of the Ombud Council is to assist in ensuring that financial
sections.title: Ombud Council's objective
sections.summary: The Ombud Council aims to ensure financial customers have access to affordable, effective, independent, and fair alternative dispute resolution processes for complaints about financial institutions regarding financial products, services, and market infrastructure services.

requirements.text: The Ombud Council's objective is to assist financial customers in accessing affordable, effective, independent, and fair alternative dispute resolution processes

sections.source_quote: 176. The objective of the Ombud Council is to assist in ensuring that financial customers have access to, and are able to use, affordable, effective, independent and fair alternative dispute resolution processes for complaints about financial institutions in relation to financial products, financial services, and services provided by market infrastructures.
sections.relevance_reason: No data compliance relevance

sections.heading: (1) In order to achieve its objective, the Ombud Council must—
sections.title: Ombud Council's functions
sections.summary: The Ombud Council must recognize industry ombud schemes, promote cooperation between ombuds, protect ombud independence, raise public awareness of ombuds, facilitate customer access to ombuds, publicize ombud schemes, resolve jurisdictional overlaps, monitor ombud performance, and support financial inclusion.

requirements.text: The Ombud Council must recognize industry ombud schemes
The Ombud Council must promote cooperation between and coordination of ombuds
The Ombud Council must strive to protect the independence and impartiality of ombuds
The Ombud Council must promote public awareness of ombuds and ombud schemes
The Ombud Council must facilitate access by financial customers to appropriate ombuds
The Ombud Council must publicize ombud schemes and the kinds of complaints they handle
The Ombud Council must resolve overlaps in the jurisdictional coverage of different ombud schemes
The Ombud Council must monitor the performance of ombud schemes
The Ombud Council must support financial inclusion

sections.source_quote: 177. (1) In order to achieve its objective, the Ombud Council must— (a) recognise, in accordance with this Chapter, industry ombud schemes; (b) promote co-operation between, and co-ordination of, the activities of ombuds; (c) strive to protect the independence and impartiality of ombuds; (d) promote public awareness of ombuds and ombud schemes and the services they provide; (e) take steps to facilitate access by financial customers to appropriate ombuds; (f) publicise ombud schemes, including publicising the kinds of complaints that different ombud schemes deal with; (g) resolve, in accordance with this Act, overlaps of the jurisdictional coverage of different ombud schemes; (h) monitor the performance of ombud schemes, including the extent to which they comply with the requirements of this Chapter and specific financial sector laws; and (i) support financial inclusion.
sections.relevance_reason: Ombud schemes not data-focused

sections.heading: The Ombud Council must—
sections.title: Ombud Council governance requirements
sections.summary: The Ombud Council must manage its affairs efficiently and implement effective governance systems and processes. This section establishes the Council's operational standards and internal oversight mechanisms to ensure accountability and effectiveness in its functions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section sets baseline governance requirements for the Ombud Council, which may indirectly affect data handling practices through its oversight role but does not directly govern data transfers or processing.

requirements.text: Manage affairs in an efficient and effective way
Establish and implement appropriate and effective governance systems and processes

sections.source_quote: 178. The Ombud Council must— (a) manage its affairs in an efficient and effective way; and (b) establish and implement appropriate and effective governance systems and processes.
sections.relevance_reason: Governance systems for Ombud Council

sections.heading: (1) The members of the Board are appointed by the Minister.
sections.title: Board appointments and eligibility
sections.summary: The Minister appoints Board members, including a Chairperson and Deputy Chairperson. Appointees cannot be ombuds, staff of ombud schemes, or individuals engaged in financial services. Board members must be ordinarily resident in the Republic and cannot be disqualified.
sections.dtia_summary: Board appointments affect data handling by ensuring only non-financial service providers can oversee complaints, reducing conflicts of interest in data processing.

sections.summary: Board members must report on compliance with financial sector laws, complaint trends, and financial institution conduct causing complaints. This section mandates transparency in how ombud schemes handle complaints and financial institutions' compliance.
