sections.heading: The Board must—
sections.title: Board responsibilities
sections.summary: The Board oversees Ombud Council management, appoints committees, sets fees, and reports on compliance, complaint trends, and financial institution conduct. This section defines the Board's operational scope and reporting duties.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates the Board to monitor financial institution conduct and complaint trends, which may influence data transfer assessments by identifying cross-border data handling risks.

requirements.text: Generally oversee management and administration of the Ombud Council to ensure efficiency and effectiveness
Appoint members of committees required or permitted by financial sector law
Give directions regarding the conduct of committee work
Make determinations of fees in terms of financial sector law
Keep the Minister informed of compliance by ombud schemes with financial sector laws
Keep the Minister informed of trends in complaints and issues raised by ombud schemes
Keep the Minister informed of how ombud schemes deal with complaint types
Keep the Minister informed of financial institution conduct causing complaints

sections.source_quote: 184. The Board must— (a) generally oversee the management and administration of the Ombud Council in order to ensure that it is efficient and effective; (b) appoint members of committees of the Ombud Council required or permitted by a law, and give directions regarding the conduct of the work of any committee; (c) make determinations of fees in terms of a financial sector law; (d) keep the Minister informed of— (i) compliance by ombud schemes with the financial sector laws in so far as they relate to ombud schemes; (ii) trends in the nature of complaints and issues raised in complaints that ombud schemes are dealing with, and how those types of issues and complaints are being dealt with; and (iii) the conduct of financial institutions that is giving rise to complaints to ombud schemes; (e) keep the financial sector regulators informed of the conduct of financial institutions that is giving rise to complaints to ombud schemes; and (f) address any other matter assigned in terms of a financial sector law to the Board.
sections.relevance_reason: Board reporting on compliance with financial laws

sections.heading: (1) (a) Tshitsinyo e e fa pele ga kopano ya Boto e nna tshwetso ya Boto fa bontsi
sections.title: Board decision validity
sections.summary: Board decisions remain valid even if there was a vacancy in the office of a member or a non-voting participant. This section ensures continuity in decision-making despite administrative gaps.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that Board decisions are valid even with vacancies or non-voting participants, which may affect data handling continuity in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: A decision of the Board is not invalid merely because there was a vacancy in the office of a member when the decision was taken
A decision of the Board is not invalid merely because a person who was not a member participated in the decision but did not vote

sections.source_quote: 186. (3) A decision of the Board is not invalid merely because— (a) there was a vacancy in the office of a member when the decision was taken; or (b) a person who was not a member participated in the decision, but did not vote.

sections.heading: (1) Motho yo e leng kgotsa yo e neng e le leloko la badirimmogo ba Khansele ya
sections.title: Disclosure of interests
sections.summary: Board members must disclose conflicts of interest, including any involvement in financial institutions or services that could affect their impartiality. This section ensures transparency in decision-making.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires disclosure of conflicts of interest, which may impact data transfer assessments by ensuring that individuals handling data have no conflicts with financial institutions.

requirements.text: Board members must disclose any interest that could affect their impartiality

sections.heading: (1) A member of the Board must disclose, at a meeting of the Board, or in writing
sections.title: Board members must disclose conflicts of interest before decision-making
sections.summary: Board members must disclose any interest in a matter being considered by them, including related party interests, to other board members. Disclosure must occur as soon as practicable after becoming aware of the interest. Board members may not perform functions related to the matter unless they have disclosed the interest and other board members have determined the interest does not affect their proper execution of duties. Similar requirements apply to committees of the Ombud Council. The disclosure must be made in writing or at a meeting. Material interests are not required to be disclosed, and the disclosure does not apply to officials of the Ombud Council or financial customers of financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: Board members must disclose conflicts of interest before participating in decisions to ensure impartiality in financial matters. This affects DTIA by requiring transparency in decision-making processes involving third parties.

requirements.text: A board member must disclose any interest in a matter being considered by them to other board members

Material interests are not required to be disclosed
Disclosure does not apply to officials of the Ombud Council or financial customers of financial institutions

sections.heading: (1) The Ombud Council must determine an application for recognition in terms
sections.title: Ombud Council must evaluate industry ombud schemes for recognition
sections.summary: The Ombud Council must determine applications for recognition of industry ombud schemes. Recognition is granted or refused based on whether the scheme meets specific criteria, including having a significant number of financial institution members, enforceable governing rules, adequate complaint handling mechanisms, sufficient resources, and alignment with public interest. The Council must also consider conditions for recognition and determine applications within three months. The evaluation ensures ombud schemes comply with financial sector laws and protect customers.
sections.dtia_summary: Ombud Council must evaluate industry ombud schemes for recognition to ensure they meet financial sector standards and protect customers, which impacts data transfer and dispute resolution processes.

Governing rules must require compliance with ombud determinations
Governing rules must provide adequate monitoring and oversight
Governing rules must require equitable treatment in complaints

Recognition must not be contrary to public interest
Ombud Council must determine applications within three months

sections.source_quote: 196. (1) The Ombud Council must determine an application for recognition in terms of section 194 by— (a) granting the application and notifying the applicant accordingly; or (b) refusing the application and notifying the applicant accordingly.

sections.heading: (1) The Ombud Council may, by notice to a recognised industry ombud scheme,
sections.title: Ombud Council may suspend recognition of industry ombud schemes
sections.summary: The Ombud Council may suspend recognition of industry ombud schemes if the scheme applies for suspension, contravenes recognition conditions, violates governing rules, provides false information, fails to comply with the Act, or if suspension is necessary to prevent serious contraventions of financial laws or material prejudice to customers. Suspension takes effect immediately upon notice and does not affect obligations under financial laws. The Council may revoke suspension at any time.
sections.dtia_summary: Ombud Council may suspend industry ombud schemes if they fail to meet compliance requirements, which impacts data transfer and dispute resolution processes.

sections.summary: The Ombud Council may develop rules for ombud schemes that are consistent with financial sector laws and do not interfere with ombud independence. The Council must seek to provide a consistent approach for all ombud schemes.

sections.heading: (1) The Ombud Council must, as soon as practicable after this Part comes into
sections.title: Establishment of financial customer complaint resolution centres
sections.summary: The Ombud Council must establish complaint resolution centres for financial customers within 30 days of this Part coming into effect. These centres provide staff and facilities to help customers formulate complaints and identify appropriate ombuds for resolution. Financial institutions may incorporate call centres into these centres to facilitate customer access.

sections.heading: (1) A financial institution may not describe any internal procedure it has for
sections.title: Financial institutions must disclose ombud schemes to customers
sections.summary: Financial institutions cannot describe internal complaint resolution procedures as ombud schemes. They must disclose applicable ombud schemes and how to contact them to customers. Financial institutions cannot require customers to submit complaints to ombuds that are not part of recognised industry or statutory ombud schemes, and such requirements are void if they violate this rule.

sections.summary: Ombud schemes and ombuds must cooperate and collaborate with each other regarding complaints about financial institutions. This includes developing joint processes to hear and determine complaints, either on their own initiative or as required by Ombud Council rules.

sections.heading: (1) Before the Ombud Council can recognise an industry ombud scheme in terms
sections.title: Ombud scheme recognition process

The governing rules must be approved by the Ombud Council and cannot be amended without their approval. The Ombud Council must ensure the rules assist in achieving the Act's objectives.

requirements.text: The Ombud Council must publish a draft of governing rules or amendments to governing rules

sections.heading: Receipt of a complaint by a financial sector regulator, the Ombud Council or an
sections.title: Complaints suspend time bars
sections.summary: Receipt of a complaint by a financial sector regulator, the Ombud Council, or an ombud suspends any applicable time barring terms, including prescription periods under the Prescription Act, 1969, from the date of complaint receipt until the complaint is withdrawn or finally determined.
sections.dtia_summary: Complaints received by regulators or ombuds suspend time bars, which may impact data transfer if complaints involve cross-border financial data.

sections.heading: (1) An ombud scheme must—
sections.title: Ombud scheme reporting obligations to Financial Sector Conduct Authority
sections.summary: An ombud scheme must submit annual reports to the Ombud Council detailing compliance with financial sector laws, complaints handled, and conduct causing complaints. It must also comply with information requests from the Ombud Council. Financial institutions must provide information to the Financial Sector Conduct Authority upon request regarding ombud schemes and financial institution conduct trends. If an ombud becomes aware of material contraventions by financial institutions or actions affecting customers, it must report to the Financial Sector Conduct Authority.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires ombud schemes to report on financial institution conduct and complaints, impacting data sharing and transparency in financial sector oversight.

Provide information to Financial Sector Conduct Authority upon request

sections.source_quote: 217. (1) An ombud scheme must— (a) within six months after the end of each financial year, submit to the Ombud Council, in the form and with the content required by the Ombud Council, a report on the operation of the ombud scheme during the financial year, including in relation to— (i) compliance with the financial sector laws in so far as they relate to ombud schemes; (ii) the complaints that the ombud scheme is dealing with, and how they are being dealt with; and (iii) the conduct of financial institutions that is giving rise to complaints; (b) comply with any request by the Ombud Council at any time for information about the operation of the ombud scheme, trends in and implications of the conduct of financial institutions observed by the ombud scheme, and any other relevant information.

sections.heading: For the purposes of this Chapter—
sections.title: Definitions of key terms for Financial Services Tribunal
sections.summary: This section defines 'decision' and 'decision-maker' for the Financial Services Tribunal. 'Decision' includes regulatory decisions, ombud decisions, market infrastructure decisions, and omissions to act. 'Decision-maker' refers to the entity responsible for decisions, such as regulators, ombud councils, or market infrastructure.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides clear definitions for decisions and decision-makers, crucial for understanding tribunal proceedings and data transfer contexts.

requirements.text: Decision includes regulatory decisions by financial sector regulators
Decision includes ombud decisions in financial sector laws
Decision includes market infrastructure decisions under Financial Markets Act
Decision includes omissions to take decisions within prescribed periods
Decision includes omissions to take decisions within reasonable periods
Decision includes actions taken as a result of decisions
Decision includes omissions to act as a result of decisions
Decision-maker refers to financial sector regulators for regulatory decisions
Decision-maker refers to Ombud Council for ombud decisions
Decision-maker refers to authorized financial services providers for market infrastructure decisions

sections.source_quote: 218. For the purposes of this Chapter— '‘decision'' means each of the following: (a) A decision by a financial sector regulator or the Ombud Council in terms of a financial sector law in relation to a specific person; (b) a decision by an authorised financial services provider, as defined in section 1 of the Financial Advisory and Intermediary Services Act, in terms of section 14 of that Act in relation to a specific person; (c) a decision in relation to a specific person by a market infrastructure, being a decision in terms of rules of the market infrastructure contemplated by the Financial Markets Act, or a decision contemplated in section 105 of the Financial Markets Act; (d) a decision of a statutory ombud in terms of a financial sector law in relation to a specific complaint by a person; (e) a decision of a kind prescribed by Regulation for the purposes of this paragraph, and includes— (f) an omission to take such a decision within the period prescribed or specified in a financial sector law, rules, or other requirements pertaining to the decision-maker; (g) an omission to take such a decision within a reasonable period, if the applicable financial sector law, or rules of, or other requirements pertaining to, the decision-maker require the decision to be taken but without prescribing or specifying a period; (h) an action taken as a result of such a decision; and (i) an omission to take action as a result of such a decision within the prescribed or a reasonable period, if the applicable financial sector law requires the action to be taken but does not prescribe a period, but does not include— (j) a decision of a financial sector regulator that the financial sector regulator is directed to take in terms of section 18(2) or 30(1); (k) a decision to conduct a supervisory on-site inspection or an investigation; (l) an assessment of a levy issued to a specific person; or (m) a decision prescribed by Regulations made for this paragraph; '‘decision-maker'' means— (a) in relation to a decision by a financial sector regulator, the financial sector regulator; (b) in relation to a decision by the Ombud Council, the Ombud Council; (c) in relation to a decision referred to in paragraph (b) of the definition of '‘decision'' in this section, the authorised financial services provider; (d) in relation to a decision referred to in paragraph (c) of the definition of '‘decision'' in this section, the market infrastructure; (e) in relation to a decision by a statutory ombud, the statutory ombud; and (f) in relation to a decision referred to in paragraph (e) of the definition of '‘decision'' in this section, the person identified in the Regulations as the decision-maker.

sections.heading: Mabapi le maitlhomo a Kgaolo eno—
sections.title: Definitions of key terms for Financial Services Tribunal
sections.summary: This section defines 'decision' and 'decision-maker' for the Financial Services Tribunal. 'Decision' includes regulatory decisions, ombud decisions, market infrastructure decisions, and omissions to act. 'Decision-maker' refers to the entity responsible for decisions, such as regulators, ombud councils, or market infrastructure.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides clear definitions for decisions and decision-makers, crucial for understanding tribunal proceedings and data transfer contexts.

requirements.text: Decision includes regulatory decisions by financial sector regulators
Decision includes ombud decisions in financial sector laws
Decision includes market infrastructure decisions under Financial Markets Act
Decision includes omissions to take decisions within prescribed periods
Decision includes omissions to take decisions within reasonable periods
Decision includes actions taken as a result of decisions
Decision includes omissions to act as a result of decisions
Decision-maker refers to financial sector regulators for regulatory decisions
Decision-maker refers to Ombud Council for ombud decisions
Decision-maker refers to authorized financial services providers for market infrastructure decisions

sections.source_quote: 218. For the purposes of this Chapter— '‘decision'' means each of the following: (a) A decision by a financial sector regulator or the Ombud Council in terms of a financial sector law in relation to a specific person; (b) a decision by an authorised financial services provider, as defined in section 1 of the Financial Advisory and Intermediary Services Act, in terms of section 14 of that Act in relation to a specific person; (c) a decision in relation to a specific person by a market infrastructure, being a decision in terms of rules of the market infrastructure contemplated by the Financial Markets Act, or a decision contemplated in section 105 of the Financial Markets Act; (d) a decision of a statutory ombud in terms of a financial sector law in relation to a specific complaint by a person; (e) a decision of a kind prescribed by Regulation for the purposes of this paragraph, and includes— (f) an omission to take such a decision within the period prescribed or specified in a financial sector law, rules, or other requirements pertaining to the decision-maker; (g) an omission to take such a decision within a reasonable period, if the applicable financial sector law, or rules of, or other requirements pertaining to, the decision-maker require the decision to be taken but without prescribing or specifying a period; (h) an action taken as a result of such a decision; and (i) an omission to take action as a result of such a decision within the prescribed or a reasonable period, if the applicable financial sector law requires the action to be taken but does not prescribe a period, but does not include— (j) a decision of a financial sector regulator that the financial sector regulator is directed to take in terms of section 18(2) or 30(1); (k) a decision to conduct a supervisory on-site inspection or an investigation; (l) an assessment of a levy issued to a specific person; or (m) a decision prescribed by Regulations made for this paragraph; '‘decision-maker'' means— (a) in relation to a decision by a financial sector regulator, the financial sector regulator; (b) in relation to a decision by the Ombud Council, the Ombud Council; (c) in relation to a decision referred to in paragraph (b) of the definition of '‘decision'' in this section, the authorised financial services provider; (d) in relation to a decision referred to in paragraph (c) of the definition of '‘decision'' in this section, the market infrastructure; (e) in relation to a decision by a statutory ombud, the statutory ombud; and (f) in relation to a decision referred to in paragraph (e) of the definition of '‘decision'' in this section, the person identified in the Regulations as the decision-maker.

sections.heading: (1) The Financial Services Tribunal is hereby established to reconsider, in terms
sections.title: Financial Services Tribunal functions and independence
sections.summary: The Financial Services Tribunal is established to reconsider decisions as defined in section 218 and perform other functions. It must be independent, impartial, and act without fear, favour, or prejudice. The Tribunal is a tribunal of record and must function in accordance with the Act and specific financial sector laws.
sections.dtia_summary: Establishes a tribunal to review financial decisions, impacting data transfer oversight and dispute resolution mechanisms.

requirements.text: Tribunal must reconsider decisions as defined in section 218
Tribunal must be independent
Tribunal must be impartial and exercise powers without fear, favour or prejudice
Tribunal must be a tribunal of record
Tribunal must perform functions in accordance with the Act and specific financial sector laws

sections.source_quote: 219. (1) The Financial Services Tribunal is hereby established to reconsider, in terms of this Chapter, decisions as defined in section 218 and to perform the other functions conferred on it by this Act and specific financial sector laws. (2) The Tribunal— (a) is independent; (b) must be impartial and exercise its powers without fear, favour or prejudice; (c) is a tribunal of record; and (d) must perform its function in accordance with this Act and the specific financial sector laws.

sections.heading: (1) Tona o tshwanetse go tlhoma le go tlamela lenane la batho ba ba
sections.title: Panel member eligibility and removal procedures
sections.summary: Panel members must be eligible to act without health or other issues, and must not have failed in their responsibilities. The Minister may remove panel members who are unable to act, have failed in responsibilities, or acted inconsistently with panel duties. Panel members must disclose interests when relevant.

sections.source_quote: 225. (1) Tona o tshwanetse go tlhoma le go tlamela lenane la batho ba ba ikemiseditseng go dira jaaka maloko a dipanele tsa Lekgotla. (2) Batho ba ba akareditsweng mo lenaneng la panele ba tshwanetse— (a) go nna le maitemogelo mo kgotsa kitso ya boitseanape— (i) ya molao; kgotsa (ii) ya dikuno tsa ditsˇhelete,ditirelo tsa ditsˇhelete, didiriso tsa ditsˇhelete, dithulaganyetso tsa popegotheo ya mmaraka kgotsa dithulaganyo tsa ditsˇhelete; le (b) go nna motho yo o itekanetseng e bile a le matshwanedi go ka akaretswa mo lenaneng la panele. (3) Motho o ka se akaretswe mo lenaneng la panele fa e le motho yo o ileditsweng. (4) Tona o ka, dingwaga dingwe le dingwe tse tlhano, laletsa mo phatlalatseng batho go dira kopo ya go akaretswa mo lenaneng la panele. (5) Modulasetilo o tshwanetse go netefatsa gore batho ba ba akareditsweng mo lenaneng la panele ba newa tsˇhono e e lekanang ya go thapiwa go dira mo paneleng ya Lekgotla.

sections.heading: An obligation in a financial sector law to notify a person of a decision taken in
sections.title: Right to request reasons for decisions
sections.summary: Financial sector laws requiring notification of decisions must include the right to request reasons for decisions as per section 229 and the right to have decisions reconsidered as per Part 4 of the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures data subjects can request reasons and reconsider decisions, impacting transparency and data transfer compliance.

requirements.text: Notification of decisions must include right to request reasons for decisions

sections.source_quote: 228. An obligation in a financial sector law to notify a person of a decision taken in relation to that person must be read as including an obligation to notify the person of that person's right— (a) to request reasons for the decision in terms of section 229; and (b) to have the decision reconsidered in terms of Part 4.
sections.relevance_reason: Right to request reasons for decisions

sections.heading: (1) A fee or levy payable to a financial sector body in terms of section 237 is a
sections.title: Fee or levy as debt payable to financial sector body
sections.summary: A fee or levy payable to a financial sector body under section 237 is classified as a debt. The financial sector body may recover this debt through judicial proceedings in a competent court. This section establishes the legal status of fees and levies as debts and outlines the recovery mechanism.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines fees and levies as debts, which may impact cross-border data transfers involving financial sector bodies if the debt recovery process affects data flows.

requirements.text: A fee or levy payable to a financial sector body in terms of section 237 is a debt due to the financial sector body
A financial sector body may recover the amount of a debt due in terms of this section by way of a judicial process in a competent court

sections.source_quote: 238. (1) A fee or levy payable to a financial sector body in terms of section 237 is a debt due to the financial sector body. (2) A financial sector body may recover the amount of a debt due in terms of this section by way of a judicial process in a competent court.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure amendment mechanics

sections.heading: (1) For each financial year, each financial sector body must prepare and adopt—
sections.title: Financial sector bodies must prepare annual budget and fee proposals
sections.summary: Each financial sector body must prepare and adopt an annual budget including expenditure estimates, fee and levy proposals, and projected expenditure for the next two financial years. The budget must account for special levies and reserves, with total reserves not exceeding 15% of total expenditure. This section mandates financial planning and transparency for financial sector bodies.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires financial sector bodies to prepare detailed financial plans, which may affect data transfer costs and compliance if financial data is involved in cross-border transactions.

requirements.text: Each financial sector body must prepare and adopt a budget in accordance with section 248 that includes an estimate of its expenditure
Each financial sector body must prepare and adopt a proposal for the fees that will be charged and levies that will be imposed by the financial sector body

A proposal for levies may include a proposal for one or more special levies, and the estimate of expenditure must include an estimate for the special expenditure in relation to a special levy proposal

An estimate of expenditure for a financial year may include provision for one or more reserves, but the total accumulated reserves included in the estimate of expenditure may not exceed 15% of the total estimated expenditure, excluding the reserves

sections.source_quote: 239. sections.title: Financial sector bodies must issue levy assessments to supervised entities
sections.summary: A financial sector body must issue to each supervised entity liable to pay a levy an assessment notice specifying the levy amount and due date, which must be at least 30 days from receipt of the notice. This section ensures timely and transparent levy collection from supervised entities.

sections.source_quote: 242. (1) A financial sector body must issue to each supervised entity that is liable to pay a levy for the financial year, an assessment of a levy payable by the supervised entity. (2) The assessment notice issued to a supervised entity must state the date on which the levy is due and must be paid, which period must not be less than 30 days from the date of receipt of the notice of assessment by the supervised entity.

sections.heading: (1) If a fee or levy is not paid, or not paid in full, within the period specified for
sections.title: Interest on late or non-payment of fees and levies
sections.summary: If a fee or levy is not paid within the specified period and instalment offers are not accepted, the person liable must pay interest at the rate prescribed in the Prescribed Rate of Interest Act, 1975. This interest is classified as a debt and may be recovered through judicial proceedings. This section addresses late payment penalties.

sections.heading: (1) (a) An auditor of a licensed financial institution, or of a holding company of
sections.title: Auditor reporting obligations to financial regulators
sections.summary: Auditors of licensed financial institutions or holding companies of financial conglomerates must promptly submit detailed written reports to the Prudential Authority regarding matters they become aware of during audits that could cause financial unsoundness, contravene financial sector laws, or prevent audit completion. These reports also include specific documents required by the Auditing Profession Act, 2005. Auditors who resign or have appointments terminated must provide written statements on reasons for resignation or termination and any pending reports they would have submitted.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires auditors to report financial risks and potential law violations to regulators, which impacts DTIA by mandating disclosure of material risks before cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions.

sections.heading: (1) A person may report to a financial sector regulator—
sections.title: Reporting to financial sector regulators without liability
sections.summary: Any person may report suspected financial difficulties, contraventions of financial sector laws, or involvement in financial crime to a financial sector regulator. Reporters are not criminally liable or liable for compensation for losses caused by the report, provided the report was not made in bad faith.

sections.source_quote: 253. sections.heading: A person may not subject another person to any prejudice in employment, or
sections.title: Protection from prejudice for whistleblowers

A person may not subject another person to any prejudice in employment, or penalise another person in any way, on the ground that the other person— (a) made a report in terms of section 252; or (b) made a report in terms of section 253, even if the report was not required by law.

sections.heading: Sections 252 and 253 apply in addition to, and do not limit, any other law that
sections.title: Reporting provisions do not limit other laws
sections.summary: Sections 252 and 253 apply in addition to and do not limit other laws that provide protection for persons reporting contraventions of financial sector laws.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures whistleblower protections under sections 252 and 253 are not overridden by other laws, which is relevant for DTIA when assessing cross-border data flows involving protected disclosures.

requirements.text: Sections 252 and 253 apply in addition to any other law providing protection for persons reporting contraventions of financial sector laws

sections.source_quote: 255. Sections 252 and 253 apply in addition to, and do not limit, any other law that provides protection for persons who properly report contraventions of the law.

sections.heading: The purpose of the Register is to provide reliable access to accurate, authoritative
sections.title: Purpose of Financial Sector Information Register
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Information Register provides reliable access to accurate, authoritative, and up-to-date information on financial sector laws, regulations, regulatory instruments, and their implementation.
sections.dtia_summary: Serves as a centralized repository for financial regulations, which impacts DTIA by requiring assessments of data storage and access mechanisms for regulatory compliance across borders.

requirements.text: Register provides reliable access to accurate, authoritative, and up-to-date information on financial sector laws
Register provides reliable access to accurate, authoritative, and up-to-date information on financial sector regulations
Register provides reliable access to accurate, authoritative, and up-to-date information on regulatory instruments

sections.source_quote: 257. The purpose of the Register is to provide reliable access to accurate, authoritative and up to date information relating to financial sector laws, Regulations, regulatory instruments and their implementation.

sections.heading: (1) The Register is, for all purposes, taken to be a complete and accurate record
sections.title: Register corrections for financial sector entities
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Director-General may correct errors in the Register. If corrections are made, the Director-General must annotate records to explain the rectification, specify the date and time of correction, and state the reason for the correction. This applies to the Register of financial sector entities maintained by the Director-General.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures transparency in the Register by requiring clear documentation of corrections, which is relevant for DTIAs involving financial data accuracy and record-keeping compliance.

requirements.text: The Director-General may arrange for the Register to be corrected to rectify errors

The Director-General must annotate relevant records in the Register to explain the nature of the rectification and specify the date and time the rectification was made and the reason for the rectification

sections.source_quote: 263. (1) The Director-General may arrange for the Register to be corrected to rectify errors. (2) If the Register is corrected, the Director-General must annotate relevant records in the Register to explain the nature of the rectification and specify the date and time the rectification was made and the reason for the rectification.

sections.heading: (1) A supervised entity that contravenes section 131(1)(b) commits an offence
sections.title: Offences for contravening financial sector regulations
sections.summary: This section outlines offences for supervised entities that contravene specific financial regulations. It specifies fines for contravening sections 131(1)(b), 132(4)(a)(iii), and other provisions, including scenarios where documents are removed or concealed without reasonable excuse. The offences apply to supervised entities and individuals.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details penalties for non-compliance with financial data handling regulations, which is relevant for DTIAs assessing risks associated with regulatory breaches and financial data protection.

requirements.text: A supervised entity that contravenes section 131(1)(b) commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R1 000 for each day during which the offence continues
A supervised entity that or person who contravenes section 132(4)(a)(iii) commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R5 000 000

If a financial sector regulator gives a directive and without reasonable excuse a business document is removed, concealed, destroyed or interfered with, the supervised entity or person commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R2 500 000
A person who contravenes section 133 commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R1 000 000 or imprisonment for a period not exceeding 12 months, or to both
A person who contravenes section 139 commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R5 000 000 or imprisonment for a period not exceeding two years, or to both

sections.source_quote: 267. (1) A supervised entity that contravenes section 131(1)(b) commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R1 000 for each day during which the offence continues. (2) A supervised entity that or person who contravenes section 132(4)(a)(iii) commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R5 000 000. (3) If— (a) a financial sector regulator gives a supervised entity a directive in terms of section 132(4)(a)(iii); and (b) without reasonable excuse, a business document to which the directive relates is removed from the premises, or concealed, destroyed or otherwise interfered with, contrary to the directive, the supervised entity or person on whom the directive was served commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R2 500 000. (4) A person who contravenes section 133 commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R1 000 000 or imprisonment for a period not exceeding 12 months, or to both a fine and such imprisonment. (5) A person who contravenes section 139 commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R5 000 000 or imprisonment for a period not exceeding two years, or to both a fine and such imprisonment.
sections.relevance_reason: Fines for record-keeping breaches

sections.heading: (1) (a) A financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank commits an offence if
sections.title: Unauthorized disclosure of financial information
sections.summary: This section prohibits unauthorized disclosure or sharing of information by financial sector regulators or the Reserve Bank. It specifies that information must be shared only for purposes authorized under section 251(1) or (2), and in a manner authorized under section 251(5). Both the person sharing information and the regulator or Reserve Bank are liable for offenses.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs the conditions under which financial information can be shared, which is critical for DTIAs assessing data sharing practices across borders and with third parties.

requirements.text: A financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank commits an offence if information is disclosed or shared for a purpose not authorized in terms of section 251(1) or (2), or in a manner not authorized as referred to in section 251(5)

Both an official or employee who shares or discloses information and the financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank on whose behalf the information is shared or disclosed commit an offence if an official or employee— (i) who is not authorized to share or disclose information shares or discloses information in contravention of section 251(6)(c); (ii) who is authorized to share or disclose information shares or discloses information for a purpose not authorized in terms of section 251(1) or (2), or in a manner that contravenes section 251(3) or (4)

sections.source_quote: 272. (1) (a) A financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank commits an offence if information is disclosed or shared for a purpose that is not authorised in terms of section 251(1) or (2), or in a manner that is not authorised as referred to in section 251(5). (b) Both an official or employee who shares or discloses information, and the financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank on whose behalf the information is shared or disclosed, commit an offence if an official or employee— (i) who is not authorised to share or disclose information shares or discloses information in contravention of section 251(6)(c); (ii) who is authorised to share or disclose information shares or discloses information for a purpose that is not authorised in terms of section 251(1) or (2), or in a manner that contravenes section 251(3) or (4).
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits unauthorized financial data sharing

sections.heading: A person who provides to a financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank,
sections.title: False or misleading information to regulators
sections.summary: This section prohibits providing false or misleading information to financial sector regulators or the Reserve Bank, including by omission. The person providing such information commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R10 000 000 or imprisonment for up to 10 years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses the consequences of providing inaccurate information to regulators, which is relevant for DTIAs assessing data accuracy and integrity in financial reporting.

requirements.text: A person who provides to a financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank, information in connection with the operation of a financial sector law, that the person knew or believed, or ought reasonably to have known or believed, to be false or misleading, including by omission, commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R10 000 000 or imprisonment for a period not exceeding 10 years, or to both a fine and such imprisonment

sections.source_quote: 273. A person who provides to a financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank, information in connection with the operation of a financial sector law, that the person knew or believed, or ought reasonably to have known or believed, to be false or misleading, including by omission, commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R10 000 000 or imprisonment for a period not exceeding 10 years, or to both a fine and such imprisonment.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator penalties for false financial data reporting

sections.heading: A person who is required in terms of a financial sector law to keep accounts or
sections.title: Accounting inaccuracies and intentional deception in financial records
sections.summary: A financial entity that must maintain accounts under financial sector law commits an offence if its records fail to accurately reflect transactions or operations. The offence requires the entity to have known or been reckless about the accuracy of the records, or intended to deceive regulators or obstruct investigations. Conviction may result in a fine up to R10 million or imprisonment for up to 10 years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes strict accountability for financial record-keeping accuracy, directly impacting data transfer assessments by requiring precise financial records to prevent regulatory non-compliance during cross-border financial data transfers.

requirements.text: Accounts or records must correctly record and explain matters, transactions, acts or operations to which they relate
The person must have known or been reckless about the accuracy of the accounts or records
The person must have intended to deceive or mislead a financial sector regulator or investigator
The person must have intended to hinder or obstruct a financial sector regulator or investigator in performing duties

sections.source_quote: A person who is required in terms of a financial sector law to keep accounts or records commits an offence if— (a) the accounts or records do not correctly record and explain the matters, transactions, acts or operations to which they relate; and (b) the person— (i) knew that, or was reckless whether, the accounts or records correctly recorded and explained the matters, transactions, acts or operations to which they relate; (ii) intended to deceive or mislead a financial sector regulator or an investigator; or (iii) intended to hinder or obstruct a financial sector regulator, or an investigator in performing his or her duties in terms of a financial sector law
sections.relevance_reason: Financial fraud penalties apply to data accuracy

sections.heading: A person who, without the consent of the financial sector regulator, applies to a
sections.title: Unauthorized use of financial regulator names in business operations
sections.summary: A person commits an offence if they apply to a company, body, business or undertaking a name or description that implies a connection to a financial sector regulator without prior consent from the regulator. The offence carries a maximum fine of R5 million.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects data transfer assessments by prohibiting misleading representations in financial entities, which could impact cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions.

requirements.text: A person may not apply to a company, body, business or undertaking a name or description that reasonably signifies or implies some connection between the company, body, business or undertaking and a financial sector regulator without consent from the financial sector regulator

sections.source_quote: A person who, without the consent of the financial sector regulator, applies to a company, body, business or undertaking a name or description that reasonably signifies or implies some connection between the company, body, business or undertaking and a financial sector regulator commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding R5 000 000.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers and penalties for financial data

sections.heading: A financial sector regulator must, if asked, assist a person to make a complaint
sections.title: Regulator assistance in financial complaints
sections.summary: Financial sector regulators must assist individuals in making complaints to the ombud regarding financial products or services when the complaint relates to actions or practices under financial sector law. This includes facilitating complaints against entities providing financial services.
sections.dtia_summary: This section supports data transfer impact assessments by ensuring regulators can assist in resolving complaints, which may be relevant when cross-border financial data transfers involve disputes or regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: A financial sector regulator must assist a person to make a complaint to the appropriate ombud about actions or practices in terms of a financial sector law of a person in connection with providing financial products or financial services

sections.source_quote: A financial sector regulator must, if asked, assist a person to make a complaint to the appropriate ombud about the actions or practices in terms of a financial sector law, of a person in connection with providing financial products or financial services.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator assistance in financial complaints

sections.heading: A person, including a financial sector regulator, who suffers loss because of a
sections.title: Recovery of losses from contraventions
sections.summary: A person, including financial sector regulators, who suffers loss due to a contravention of financial sector law by another person may recover the loss through legal action against the contravening party or any knowingly involved party. This applies to both individuals and entities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for data transfer impact assessments as it addresses liability for financial losses arising from data breaches or non-compliance, which could impact cross-border financial data transfers.

requirements.text: A person who suffers loss because of a contravention of a financial sector law by another person may recover the amount of the loss by action in a court of competent jurisdiction against the other person

A person who suffers loss because of a contravention of a financial sector law by another person may recover the amount of the loss by action in a court of competent jurisdiction against any person who was knowingly involved in the contravention

sections.source_quote: A person, including a financial sector regulator, who suffers loss because of a contravention of a financial sector law by another person, may recover the amount of the loss by action in a court of competent jurisdiction against— (a) the other person; and (b) any person who was knowingly involved in the contravention.
sections.relevance_reason: Recovery of financial losses from contraventions

sections.heading: (1) A financial sector regulator may, for a valid reason, extend any period for
sections.title: Extensions for compliance periods
sections.summary: Financial sector regulators may extend compliance periods for financial sector laws, except for provisions they must comply with, for valid reasons. Extensions can be granted multiple times, either before or after the original deadline.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts data transfer impact assessments by allowing extended compliance periods for financial data transfers, which may affect timelines for cross-border data transfers and regulatory alignment.

requirements.text: A financial sector regulator may extend any period for compliance with, or a period prescribed by, a provision of a financial sector law, other than a provision that the financial sector regulator must comply with, for a valid reason

A financial sector regulator may grant an extension in terms of subsection (1) more than once, and may do so either before or after the time for compliance has passed or the period prescribed has ended

sections.source_quote: (1) A financial sector regulator may, for a valid reason, extend any period for compliance with, or a period prescribed by, a provision of a financial sector law, other than a provision that the financial sector regulator must comply with. (2) A financial sector regulator may grant an extension in terms of subsection (1) more than once, and may do so either before or after the time for compliance has passed or the period prescribed has ended.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator extensions for financial compliance periods

sections.heading: (1) A licence may be given subject to conditions specified in the licence or in the
sections.title: Conditions for financial licences
sections.summary: Licences for financial entities may be granted subject to conditions specified in the licence or the notice of grant. Suspension, cancellation, or revocation of licences may also be subject to conditions in the notice given to the licensee. Contravention of conditions does not affect the licence status.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for data transfer impact assessments as it establishes conditions for financial licences, which may impact cross-border data transfers through regulatory requirements for financial entities.

requirements.text: A licence may be given subject to conditions specified in the licence or in the notice of the grant or issue of the licence given to the licensee

A suspension, cancellation or revocation of a licence in terms of a financial sector law may be subject to conditions specified in the notice of the suspension, cancellation or revocation given to the licensee
Contravention of a condition in terms of subsection (2) does not affect the suspension, cancellation or revocation of the licence

sections.source_quote: (1) A licence may be given subject to conditions specified in the licence or in the notice of the grant or issue of the licence given to the licensee. (2) A suspension, cancellation or revocation of a licence in terms of a financial sector law may be subject to conditions specified in the notice of the suspension, cancellation or revocation given to the licensee. (3) Contravention of a condition in terms of subsection (2) does not affect the suspension, cancellation or revocation of the licence.
sections.relevance_reason: Conditions for financial licences affect data compliance via financial data handling

sections.heading: (1) The responsible authority for a financial sector law may, in writing and with
sections.title: Exemptions from financial sector laws with public interest and objectives
sections.summary: The responsible authority for a financial sector law may exempt persons or classes from specified provisions unless it determines that granting the exemption would be contrary to public interest or prejudice the achievement of the financial sector law's objectives. This exemption power applies only when the financial sector law lacks a specific exemption mechanism. The authority must grant exemptions in accordance with the law and publish them.
sections.dtia_summary: Exemptions from financial sector laws require public interest assessment and alignment with law objectives, impacting cross-border data transfers if financial institutions seek exemptions for international data handling.
sections.authority: Responsible authority for a financial sector law

requirements.text: The responsible authority may exempt any person or class of persons from a specified provision of the financial sector law
The responsible authority must consider that granting the exemption will be contrary to the public interest
The responsible authority must consider that granting the exemption may prejudice the achievement of the objects of a financial sector law
The exemption applies only if the financial sector law does not provide a power to grant exemptions
The responsible authority must grant the exemption in terms of the relevant provisions of the financial sector law
The responsible authority must publish each exemption

sections.source_quote: 281. (1) The responsible authority for a financial sector law may, in writing and with the concurrence of the other financial sector regulator, exempt any person or class of persons from a specified provision of the financial sector law, unless it considers that granting the exemption— (a) will be contrary to the public interest; or (b) may prejudice the achievement of the objects of a financial sector law.

sections.heading: (1) If this Act provides that a financial sector regulator must notify the other
sections.title: Exemption and concurrence notifications via memoranda
sections.summary: Financial sector regulators may avoid notifying or seeking concurrence from other regulators if they have a memorandum of understanding that failure to notify does not prejudice objectives or that notification is unnecessary. This applies to both notification requirements and concurrence needs for actions under financial sector laws.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulators can bypass notification or concurrence for cross-border data transfers if mutual agreements exist that actions won't harm objectives or require coordination.

requirements.text: If a financial sector regulator must notify another regulator of a matter, notification is not required if the other regulator has agreed that failure to provide notice does not prejudice its objective
If a financial sector regulator must notify another regulator of a matter, notification is not required if the other regulator has agreed that the notification is unnecessary
If a financial sector regulator must seek concurrence for an action, concurrence is not required if the other regulator has agreed that the action does not prejudice its objective
If a financial sector regulator must seek concurrence for an action, concurrence is not required if the other regulator has agreed that its concurrence is unnecessary
If a financial sector regulator must seek concurrence from the Reserve Bank, concurrence is not required if the Reserve Bank has agreed that concurrence is unnecessary

sections.source_quote: 282. (1) If this Act provides that a financial sector regulator must notify the other financial sector regulator of a particular matter, the notification is not required if the other regulator has agreed, in a section 77 memorandum of understanding or otherwise, that— (a) failure to provide the notice does not prejudice the achievement of its objective; and (b) the notification is unnecessary.
sections.relevance_reason: regulator coordination for financial data

sections.heading: Each of the financial sector regulators and the Ombud Council must establish
sections.title: Regulators must establish consultation arrangements with financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial sector regulators and the Ombud Council must establish and implement arrangements to facilitate consultation and information exchange with financial institutions, customers, and prospective customers on matters of mutual interest. This ensures transparency and stakeholder engagement in financial sector operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulators must create mechanisms for cross-border data sharing with stakeholders, impacting data transfer impact assessments for international financial institutions.
sections.authority: Financial sector regulators and Ombud Council

requirements.text: Each financial sector regulator and the Ombud Council must establish arrangements to facilitate consultation

Each financial sector regulator and the Ombud Council must give effect to arrangements for exchange of information with financial institutions, financial customers, and prospective financial customers

sections.source_quote: 283. Each of the financial sector regulators and the Ombud Council must establish and give effect to arrangements to facilitate consultation and the exchange of information with financial institutions, financial customers, and prospective financial customers on matters of mutual interest.
sections.relevance_reason: tangential governance for financial sector

sections.heading: In any proceedings in terms of, or in relation to, a financial sector law, the records
sections.title: Financial institution records admissible as evidence
sections.summary: Records and books of account of financial institutions and their control function employees are admissible as evidence in proceedings related to financial sector laws. This ensures transparency and accountability in financial transactions and regulatory oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institution records are admissible as evidence in cross-border disputes, affecting data retention and transfer impact assessments for international financial data.

requirements.text: Records and books of account of a financial institution are admissible as evidence in proceedings in terms of or in relation to a financial sector law

Records and books of account of a person engaged by a financial institution to perform a control function are admissible as evidence in proceedings in terms of or in relation to a financial sector law

sections.source_quote: 284. In any proceedings in terms of, or in relation to, a financial sector law, the records and books of account of a financial institution, and of a person who is engaged by a financial institution to perform a control function, are admissible as evidence of the matters, transactions and accounts recorded therein.
sections.relevance_reason: Records admissible as evidence in financial proceedings

sections.heading: The State, the Minister, the Reserve Bank, the Governor and Deputy Governors,
sections.title: Immunities for financial sector officials
sections.summary: State officials, regulators, Reserve Bank members, and financial sector regulators are immune from liability for losses or damage arising from decisions or actions taken in good faith while exercising their functions under financial sector laws. This protects officials from legal consequences related to financial operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Immunities for financial officials protect cross-border data handling decisions, reducing legal risks in international financial data transfers.

requirements.text: The State, the Minister, the Reserve Bank, the Governor and Deputy Governors, a financial sector regulator, a member of the Executive Committee, the Prudential Committee, a member of a subcommittee of the Prudential Authority or the Financial Sector Conduct Authority, a member of the Tribunal, the Ombud Council, a member of the Ombud Board, an employee of the State, a board member or officer of the Reserve Bank, a staff member of a financial sector regulator, a staff member of the Reserve Bank, a person appointed by a financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank to exercise a power or perform a function or duty in terms of a financial sector law are not liable for loss or damage

sections.source_quote: 285. The State, the Minister, the Reserve Bank, the Governor and Deputy Governors, a financial sector regulator, a member of the Executive Committee, the Prudential Committee, a member of a subcommittee of the Prudential Authority or the Financial Sector Conduct Authority, a member of the Tribunal, the Ombud Council, a member of the Ombud Board, an employee of the State, a board member or officer of the Reserve Bank, a staff member of a financial sector regulator, a staff member of the Reserve Bank, a person appointed by a financial sector regulator or the Reserve Bank to exercise a power or perform a function or duty in terms of a financial sector law is not liable for, or in respect of, any loss or damage suffered or incurred by any person arising from a decision taken or action performed in good faith in the exercise of a function, power or duty in terms of a financial sector law.
sections.relevance_reason: Immunities for officials not relevant to data compliance

sections.heading: (1) The Minister may make Regulations to facilitate the implementation of this
sections.title: Regulation publication and consultation process for financial sector laws
sections.summary: The Minister may issue regulations to implement the Act, including those prescribing requirements or addressing procedural matters. Regulations must be published in the Gazette and Register. The Minister must follow a consultation process involving drafting, public consultation, submission to Parliament, and reporting on the consultation. The consultation period must be at least six weeks, and parliamentary scrutiny must be at least 30 days. If the consultation process leads to prejudice or harm, the Minister must publish a draft and statement explaining the delay.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates a formal consultation process for regulations affecting financial institutions, requiring transparency and stakeholder input before implementation. It impacts DTIAs by requiring regulators to document and justify regulatory changes to ensure they do not harm financial customers or the system.

requirements.text: The Minister may make Regulations to facilitate the implementation of this Act
Regulations must be published in the Gazette and Register
The Minister must publish a draft of the Regulation and a statement explaining the need and intended operation
The Minister must publish a statement of the expected impact of the Regulation
The Minister must publish a notice inviting submissions on the Regulation
The Minister must submit to Parliament the documents from the consultation process
The consultation period must be at least six weeks
The parliamentary scrutiny period must be at least 30 days
If compliance with consultation process leads to prejudice or harm, the Minister must publish a draft and statement explaining the delay

sections.source_quote: 288. sections.heading: (1) Tona o ka dira Melawana go nolofatsa go tsenngwa mo tirisong ga Molao
sections.title: Regulation publication and consultation process for financial sector laws
sections.summary: The Minister may issue regulations to implement the Act, including those prescribing requirements or addressing procedural matters. Regulations must be published in the Gazette and Register. The Minister must follow a consultation process involving drafting, public consultation, submission to Parliament, and reporting on the consultation. The consultation period must be at least six weeks, and parliamentary scrutiny must be at least 30 days. If the consultation process leads to prejudice or harm, the Minister must publish a draft and statement explaining the delay.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates a formal consultation process for regulations affecting financial institutions, requiring transparency and stakeholder input before implementation. It impacts DTIAs by requiring regulators to document and justify regulatory changes to ensure they do not harm financial customers or the system.

sections.title: Council for Medical Schemes replaces Prudential Authority and Financial Sector Conduct Authority
sections.summary: The Council for Medical Schemes can exercise the Prudential Authority's functions and powers related to medical schemes instead of the Prudential Authority, with the Prudential Authority's concurrence. Similarly, the Council for Medical Schemes can exercise the Financial Sector Conduct Authority's functions and powers related to medical schemes instead of the Financial Sector Conduct Authority, with the Financial Sector Conduct Authority's concurrence. Any determination must be published, and concurrence is not required if the regulator agrees in writing that the exercise does not prejudice its objectives.

sections.title: Financial Sector Conduct Authority assumes prudential powers for specific institutions
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Conduct Authority assumes the power to make prudential standards for collective investment schemes, pension funds, and friendly societies. These standards must align with specific financial sector laws and cannot impose requirements beyond what is relevant to the institution. The Financial Sector Conduct Authority may exercise other powers to achieve the Prudential Authority's objectives without affecting its powers over the institution.

sections.title: Staff transfer and pension fund responsibilities between financial regulators

sections.title: Substitution of parties in pending proceedings
sections.summary: This section requires the Prudential Authority to be substituted as a party in pending proceedings for the Reserve Bank or a registrar under specified financial sector laws when the section comes into effect. Similarly, the Financial Sector Conduct Authority must be substituted as a party for the Financial Services Board or relevant registrars in pending proceedings that have not been finally determined.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by requiring substitution of parties in pending proceedings, which may impact cross-border data transfers if the proceedings involve international entities or data flows.
sections.authority: Prudential Authority, Financial Sector Conduct Authority

requirements.text: The Prudential Authority must be substituted as a party in any pending proceedings that have been commenced but not finally determined immediately before the date on which this section comes into effect for the Reserve Bank or a registrar in terms of the Banks Act, the Mutual Banks Act, 1993, the Co-operative Banks Act, 2007, the Short-term Insurance Act or the Long-term Insurance Act.

The Financial Sector Conduct Authority must be substituted as a party in any pending proceedings that have been commenced but not finally determined immediately before the date on which this section comes into effect for the Financial Services Board, the Directorate of Market Abuse where applicable, or a registrar in terms of a financial sector law other than the Banks Act.

sections.heading: (1) A licence, authorisation, approval, registration, consent or similar permission
sections.title: Continuation of permissions and regulatory instruments
sections.summary: This section states that licenses, authorizations, approvals, registrations, consents, and similar permissions given under financial sector laws remain in force but may be amended or revoked by the responsible authority. It also covers rules and regulatory instruments that continue in force with the possibility of amendment or revocation by the relevant authority.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it addresses the continuity of permissions and regulatory instruments, which may impact data transfers and processing activities across borders.
sections.authority: Responsible authorities for financial sector laws

requirements.text: Licenses, authorizations, approvals, registrations, consents or similar permissions given in terms of a financial sector law and in force immediately before the date on which this section comes into effect remain in force for the purposes of the financial sector law but may be amended or revoked by the responsible authority for the financial sector law.

Rules made in terms of section 26 of the Financial Advisory and Intermediary Services Act and in force immediately before the date on which this section comes into effect have effect as Ombud Council rules and may be amended or revoked by Ombud Council rules in accordance with this Act.

Regulatory instruments or Regulations made or issued in terms of a financial sector law and in force immediately before the date on which this section comes into effect remain in force for the purposes of the financial sector law but may be amended or revoked by a regulatory instrument made by the responsible authority for the financial sector law in accordance with the relevant financial sector law.

Consultations undertaken before the date on which Part 1 of Chapter 7 comes into effect in relation to a regulatory instrument proposed to be made under a specific financial sector law or a proposed financial sector law after that Part came into effect are taken to meet the requirements of this Act for consultation to the extent that they meet the requirements of the specific financial sector law for consultation prior to the amendment of that law in accordance with Schedule 4.

Consultations undertaken before the date on which Part 1 of Chapter 7 comes into effect in relation to a regulatory instrument proposed to be made under a specific financial sector law or a proposed financial sector law after that Part came into effect are taken to meet the requirements of this Act for consultation to the extent that they substantially meet the requirements of this Act for consultation on the proposed regulatory instrument.

Regulations made in terms of section 5 of the Financial Supervision of the Road Accident Fund Act, 1993 and in force on the date on which this section comes into effect continue in force, but may be amended or repealed by Regulations made in terms of section 5 by the Prudential Authority.

An ombud scheme that, immediately before the repeal of the Financial Services Ombuds Schemes Act, 2004 came into effect, was recognised in terms of that Act must be taken to be a recognised industry ombud scheme as if it had been recognised under this Act.

Subsection (6) ceases to have effect at the end of 12 months after Chapter 14 takes effect, but the Ombud Council may, on application and for good reason, extend the application of that subsection in a particular case for a further period of not more than 6 months.

sections.title: Amendment of section 3 for regulatory consistency
sections.summary: This section amends section 3 to specify that the Financial Sector Regulation Act prevails over other national legislation except for the Financial Intelligence Centre Act and the Financial Sector Regulation Act itself, and that powers or duties conferred by other legislation must be exercised in consultation with the Registrar Authority.

sections.heading: The amendment of section 83—
sections.title: Financial Sector Regulation Act alignment with other laws
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Financial Sector Regulation Act aligns with the Financial Sector Regulation Act. It requires that any provision of the Act that refers to a 'regulatory instrument' must be interpreted as a matter prescribed by the Authority where notice in the Gazette is specifically required. The section also specifies that references to inspections, prescribed fees, and reviews of decisions must be read as investigations, relevant fees determined under section 237, and reconsiderations by the Tribunal, respectively.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies how the Financial Sector Regulation Act interacts with other regulatory instruments and procedures, which is relevant for DTIAs when aligning data transfer mechanisms with existing regulatory frameworks.

A reference in this Act to a prescribed fee must be read as a reference to the relevant fee determined in terms of section 237 and Chapter 16 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
A reference in this Act to a review of a decision of the Authority must be read as a reference to a reconsideration of the decision by the Tribunal

sections.heading: The amendment of section 18—
sections.title: Licensing requirements for market infrastructure operators
sections.summary: This section mandates that persons operating as exchanges, central securities depositories, trade repositories, clearing houses, or central counterparties must be licensed under specified sections. Unlicensed entities cannot purport to be such entities or use names suggesting connections to licensed market infrastructure operators. The section defines prohibited activities for unlicensed persons to prevent market manipulation and ensure regulatory oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes licensing requirements for market infrastructure operators, which directly impacts cross-border data transfers by requiring licensed entities to handle financial data under specific regulatory frameworks. Unlicensed entities face restrictions on using names or descriptions that suggest market infrastructure connections.

sections.heading: The substitution in section 26 for sub-
sections.title: Supervisory cooperation arrangements for financial market infrastructure
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Financial Sector Regulator (FSR) must establish supervisory cooperation with equivalent foreign authorities. It requires specific mechanisms for information exchange, breach notifications, coordination of inspections, and handling of supervisory information for market infrastructure entities. The cooperation must cover international market infrastructure licenses and ensure timely responses to regulatory concerns.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires international supervisory cooperation arrangements for financial market infrastructure, including information exchange protocols and breach notification mechanisms that impact cross-border data transfers and regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: The Authority must enter into a supervisory co-operation arrangement with the relevant supervisory authority from the equivalent jurisdiction
The supervisory co-operation arrangement must specify mechanisms for information exchange between the Authority, South African Reserve Bank, Prudential Authority, and relevant supervisory authorities
The supervisory co-operation arrangement must specify mechanisms for prompt notification to the Authority when a supervisory authority deems an external market infrastructure in breach
The supervisory co-operation arrangement must specify procedures for coordination of supervisory activities including cross-border on-site inspections
The supervisory co-operation arrangement must specify processes for using supervisory information for law enforcement purposes
The supervisory co-operation arrangement must specify procedures for cooperation including examination reports and document analysis
The Authority and supervisory authorities must establish confidential safeguards for non-public supervisory information
The Authority and supervisory authorities must share risk analysis assessments to identify and mitigate market risks
The Authority and supervisory authorities must share information on entities of systemic significance
The Authority and supervisory authorities must cooperate in routine oversight of internationally active licensed market infrastructure
The Authority must provide advance notification regarding issues that may affect regulatory interests of other authorities
The Authority must design mechanisms for supervisory cooperation during crisis periods
The Authority and supervisory authorities must maintain ongoing staff communications regarding international market infrastructure

sections.source_quote: 6C. (1) The Authority must enter into a supervisory co-operation arrangement with the relevant supervisory authority from the equivalent jurisdiction for the purpose of performing its functions in terms of this Act.

sections.heading: The amendment of the arrangement
sections.title: Central securities depository operational requirements
sections.summary: This section amends section 29 by requiring that a central securities depository licence specifies the registered office and operational locations. It mandates that the depository may not operate at any other location without joint prior written approval from the Registrar Authority, Prudential Authority, and South African Reserve Bank. This ensures oversight of depository operations across multiple jurisdictions.

sections.title: Definition amendments for financial sector conduct authority

sections.heading: The insertion after section 1 of the
sections.title: Financial Sector Regulation Act alignment with other laws
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Financial Sector Regulation Act aligns with the Financial Sector Regulation Act. It requires that any provision of the Act that refers to a 'regulatory instrument' must be interpreted as a matter prescribed by the Authority where notice in the Gazette is specifically required. The section also specifies that references to inspections, prescribed fees, and reviews of decisions must be read as investigations, relevant fees determined under section 237, and reconsiderations by the Tribunal, respectively.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies how the Financial Sector Regulation Act interacts with other regulatory instruments and procedures, which is relevant for DTIAs when aligning data transfer mechanisms with existing regulatory frameworks.

requirements.text: A reference in this Act to an inspection must be read as a reference to an investigation in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
A reference in this Act to the Authority publishing information on a website must be read as a reference to publishing in the Register
The Authority may also publish information or documents on its website
A reference in this Act to a prescribed fee must be read as a reference to the relevant fee determined in terms of section 237 and Chapter 16 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
A reference in this Act to a review of a decision of the Authority must be read as a reference to a reconsideration of the decision by the Tribunal

sections.source_quote: A reference in this Act to an inspection in terms of a provision of this Act must be read as a reference to an investigation in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act.

sections.heading: Artikels 8, 9 en 10 word geskrap.
sections.title: Exemptions for market infrastructure operators
sections.summary: This section outlines conditions for exemptions from licensing requirements for market infrastructure operators. Applicants must demonstrate that their operations do not conflict with public interest, frustrate the Act's objectives, or cause financial hardship to applicants or clients. The exemption process requires concurrence from the South African Reserve Bank and Prudential Authority, and includes specific criteria for external market infrastructure.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details conditions for exemptions from licensing requirements, which are critical for data transfer impact assessments involving market infrastructure operators. Exemptions require demonstrating no conflict with public interest, no frustration of Act objectives, and no financial hardship to applicants or clients.
