sections.heading: The amendment of section 30(2) by
sections.title: Systemic risk reporting obligations
sections.summary: This section amends section 30(2) by requiring entities to inform the Registrar Authority as soon as they become aware of matters that may pose systemic risk to financial markets. The obligation applies to entities that reasonably believe such matters could increase systemic risk, ensuring timely reporting to regulators for market stability.

requirements.text: Must inform the Registrar Authority of any matter that it reasonably believes may give rise to or increase systemic risk as soon as it becomes aware thereof

sections.source_quote: must, as soon as it becomes aware thereof, inform the [registrar] Authority of any matter that it reasonably believes may [pose systemic risk to the financial markets] give rise to, or increase, systemic risk;
sections.relevance_reason: Systemic risk reporting for financial data

sections.heading: kuchitjiyelwa kwesigaba 35—
sections.title: Central securities depository participant approvals
sections.summary: This section amends section 35 by requiring that central securities depositories must identify supervisory authorities and relevant laws for external participants. It mandates that depository rules may provide for approval of external participants or external central securities depositories, subject to certain conditions including regulatory oversight and legal compliance.

requirements.text: The depository rules may provide for the approval of external participants or external central securities depositories to be participants of the central securities depository
The identification of the supervisory authority that supervises that external central securities depository

The identification of the relevant laws or depository rules that apply to each aspect of the participation, including, but not limited to, the laws regulating effectiveness against third parties and insolvency proceedings

sections.source_quote: The [registrar] Authority must publish a notice of an application for an amendment of the terms of a central securities depository licence and the conditions subject to which the licence was granted in two national newspapers at the expense of the applicant and on the [official] Authority's website.
sections.relevance_reason: regulatory oversight for financial data

sections.heading: The amendment of section 36 by the
sections.title: Central securities depository registration requirements
sections.summary: This section amends section 36 by requiring that securities held by a central securities depository must be registered in the name of the depository or its wholly owned subsidiary, unless they are bearer instruments, money market securities, or recorded in an uncertificated securities register as per the Companies Act. This ensures clear ownership and regulatory oversight of securities.

requirements.text: Any securities held by a central securities depository in its central securities account must, unless they are bearer instruments, money market securities or recorded in an uncertificated securities register in accordance with section 50 of the Companies Act and the depository rules, be registered in the name of that central securities depository or its wholly owned subsidiary
The registration must be approved by the Registrar Authority

sections.source_quote: The [registrar] Authority may [direct] determine that any securities held by a central securities depository in its central securities account must, unless they are bearer instruments, money market securities or recorded in a uncertificated securities register in accordance with section 50 of the Companies Act and the depository rules, be registered in the name of that central securities depository or its wholly owned subsidiary, as defined in section 1 of the Companies Act, and approved by the [registrar] Authority.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator approval for financial data registration

sections.heading: The amendment of section 39 by the
sections.title: Amendment to securities interest provisions
sections.summary: This section amends subsection (3) of section 39 to define when an interest in uncertificated securities may be granted. The interest must be effective against third parties in relation to a central securities account or securities account where it extends to all uncertificated securities standing to the credit at the time of the pledge. The provision applies to depositary rules and covers the scope of such interests.
sections.dtia_summary: This amendment clarifies the conditions under which pledges of uncertificated securities can be effective against third parties, impacting cross-border transfers of securities interests where depositary rules apply.

requirements.text: An interest in respect of uncertificated securities may be granted where applicable and in the manner provided for in the depositary rules
The interest is effective against third parties in relation to a central securities account or securities account
The interest extends to all uncertificated securities standing to the credit of the relevant account at the time the pledge is effected

sections.source_quote: An interest in respect of uncertificated securities may be granted under this section, where applicable, and in the manner provided for in the depositor rules, and is effective against third parties, in relation to a central securities account or a securities account, where such an interest extends to all uncertificated securities standing to the credit of the relevant central securities account or securities account at the time the pledge is effected.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: securities interest rules not directly data protection

sections.heading: kuchitjiyelwa kwesigaba 47—
sections.title: Amendment to clearing house licensing requirements
sections.summary: This section amends section 47 by substituting various provisions related to clearing house and central counterparty licensing. It specifies that applications must include certain documents, fees, and rules that comply with specific sections of the Act. The amendments require applicants to provide proposed clearing house rules that meet section 53 requirements and specify publication requirements on the Authority's website.
sections.dtia_summary: This amendment requires proposed clearing house rules to be published on the Authority's website for public comments, which impacts data sharing and transparency in financial systems.

requirements.text: An application for a clearing house licence or central counterparty licence must be processed
The application fee must be determined in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
A copy of proposed clearing house rules must comply with section 53
Proposed clearing house rules must be published on the Authority's website for public comments

sections.source_quote: An application for a clearing house licence or central counterparty licence must be processed, the application fee must be determined in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act, and a copy of proposed clearing house rules must comply with section 53.

The Authority may grant a licence with the concurrence of the Prudential Authority and South African Reserve Bank after considering objections and subject to conditions the Authority deems appropriate.

sections.heading: The amendment of section 49—
sections.title: State organs must consider financial stability in operations
sections.summary: State organs other than financial sector regulators must consider financial stability implications when performing their functions and provide assistance to the Reserve Bank and Financial Stability Oversight Committee as requested to maintain financial stability. This section establishes a duty for state organs to support financial stability efforts through cooperation with regulatory bodies.
sections.dtia_summary: State organs must consider financial stability when operating, which could affect cross-border financial stability assessments for institutions operating in multiple jurisdictions.

requirements.text: State organs must consider financial stability implications when performing their functions
State organs must provide assistance and information to the Reserve Bank and Financial Stability Oversight Committee as requested

sections.source_quote: 28. An organ of state, other than a financial sector regulator, must— (a) in performing its functions, have regard to the implications of its activities on financial stability; and (b) provide such assistance and information to the Reserve Bank and the Financial Stability Oversight Committee so as to maintain and restore financial stability as the Bank or the Committee may reasonably request.

sections.heading: Kufakwa kwalesigaba lesilandzelako
sections.title: Designation of systemically important financial institutions
sections.summary: The Governor may designate financial institutions as systemically important financial institutions through written notice. This power cannot be delegated. Before designation, the Governor must notify the Financial Stability Oversight Committee and invite the institution to submit comments. The Governor must consider factors including size, complexity, interconnectedness, substitutes, Committee recommendations, and submissions when making designations. Designations may be revoked and must be published.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs the designation of systemically important financial institutions, which affects cross-border data transfers for institutions in high-risk categories.

requirements.text: Governor may designate financial institutions as systemically important financial institutions via written notice
Governor's power to designate cannot be delegated
Governor must notify Financial Stability Oversight Committee and invite institution to submit comments before designation
Governor must consider size, complexity, interconnectedness, substitutes, Committee recommendations, and submissions when designating
Designation must be published
Governor may revoke designations in writing

sections.source_quote: 29. (1) (a) The Governor may, by written notice to a financial institution, designate the institution as a systemically important financial institution.
sections.relevance_reason: irrelevant: systemically important financial institutions designation

sections.heading: Kuchitjiyelwa kwesigaba 50—
sections.title: Financial institution steps requiring Reserve Bank approval
sections.summary: This section mandates that certain actions by financial institutions require explicit approval from the Reserve Bank. These actions include the sale of personal information, disclosure of personal information to third parties, transfer of personal information, amalgamation or merger with another company, and entering into compromise arrangements with creditors. The Reserve Bank may invalidate any step taken without its concurrence.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must obtain Reserve Bank approval before taking specific actions involving personal data transfers or structural changes, which impacts cross-border data transfers and institutional restructuring.

requirements.text: Go sekega, go fetola, go tlhabolola kgotsa go phimola laesense e e reboletsweng setheo seo sa ditsˇhelete
Go dirisa tharabololo e e kgethegileng go konotelela setheo sa ditsˇhelete ka boithaopo
Go dira kopo go kgotlatshekelo ya gore setheo sa ditsˇhelete se konotelelwe
Go thapa motsamaisi, motlhokomedi kgotsa motlhomamisi mo setheong sa ditsˇhelete
Go baya setheo sa ditsˇhelete ka fa tlase ga phaloso ya kgwebo kgotsa tiriso ya leano la phaloso ya kgwebo mo setheong sa ditsˇhelete
Entering into an agreement for amalgamation or merger of the financial institution with a company
Entering into a compromise arrangement with creditors of the financial institution
A step referred to in subsection (1) that is taken without the Reserve Bank's concurrence is void

sections.source_quote: 31. (1) Ga go epe ya dikgato tse di latelang e e ka tsewang mabapi le setheo sa ditsˇhelete se se botlhokwa mo thulaganyong kgotsa setheo sa ditsˇhelete ditsˇhelete se se botlhokwa mo thulaganyong mo gare ga ditheo tsa ditsˇhelete tse di kopantsweng ntle le thebolo ya Banka ya Resefe: (a) Go sekega, go fetola, go tlhabolola kgotsa go phimola laesense e e reboletsweng setheo seo sa ditsˇhelete; (b) go dirisa tharabololo e e kgethegileng go konotelela setheo sa ditsˇhelete ka boithaopo; (c) go dira kopo go kgotlatshekelo ya gore setheo sa ditsˇhelete se konotelelwe; (d) go thapa motsamaisi, motlhokomedi kgotsa motlhomamisi mo setheong sa ditsˇhelete; (e) go baya setheo sa ditsˇhelete ka fa tlase ga phaloso ya kgwebo kgotsa tiriso ya leano la phaloso ya kgwebo mo setheong sa ditsˇhelete; (f) entering into an agreement for amalgamation or merger of the financial institution with a company; and (g) entering into a compromise arrangement with creditors of the financial institution. (2) A step referred to in subsection (1) that is taken without the Reserve Bank's concurrence is void.
sections.relevance_reason: Reserve Bank approval for cross-border data transfers

sections.heading: The amendment of section 51—
sections.title: Establishment of Prudential Authority
sections.summary: This section establishes the Prudential Authority as a juristic person operating under the Reserve Bank's administration. It clarifies that the Prudential Authority is not a public entity under the Public Finance Management Act. The section defines the authority's legal status and relationship with the Reserve Bank.
sections.dtia_summary: The Prudential Authority is established as a regulatory body under the Reserve Bank, which may impact data governance structures and oversight mechanisms for financial institutions.

requirements.text: An authority called the Prudential Authority is hereby established
The Prudential Authority is a juristic person operating within the administration of the Reserve Bank
The Prudential Authority is not a public entity in terms of the Public Finance Management Act

sections.source_quote: 32. (1) An authority called the Prudential Authority is hereby established. (2) The Prudential Authority is a juristic person operating within the administration of the Reserve Bank. (3) The Prudential Authority is not a public entity in terms of the Public Finance Management Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure governance structure, no data compliance relevance

sections.heading: The amendment of section 53—
sections.title: Amendment of clearing house rules to align with financial regulations
sections.summary: This section amends subsection (1) of section 53 to require clearing house rules to be consistent with the Financial Sector Regulation Act, the Financial Sector Regulation Act, and any standards made under those acts. It also modifies subsection (2) to include specific provisions regarding insolvency proceedings, segregation of funds and securities, and notification requirements for clearing members.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates that clearing house rules must align with financial regulations, impacting cross-border data transfers by requiring consistency with relevant standards and procedures for insolvency and segregation.

requirements.text: Clearing house rules must be consistent with this Act, the Financial Sector Regulation Act and any standard made in terms of this Act or the Financial Sector Regulation Act
For the administration of securities and funds held for own account or on behalf of a client by a clearing member, including settlement of unsettled transactions under insolvency proceedings
For the segregation and portability of funds and securities held as collateral
Clearing members must notify the clearing house as soon as it commences an insolvency proceeding or an insolvency proceeding is commenced against it
In the case of a central counterparty, for the default procedures to be followed, including close-out procedures, in the event of a default of a clearing member

sections.source_quote: 34. The amendment of section 53— (a) by the substitution for subsection (1) of the following subsection: '‘(1) The clearing house rules must be consistent with this Act, the Financial Sector Regulation Act and any standard made in terms of this Act or the Financial Sector Regulation Act.'';
sections.relevance_reason: Clearing rules align with financial regulations and insolvency procedures

sections.heading: Kuchitjiyelwa kwesigaba 55—
sections.title: Amendment of section 55 to include regulatory oversight mechanisms
sections.summary: This section amends section 55 to require the Registrar Authority to have powers to verify information, consider additional information from various sources, and prescribe detailed requirements for applicants. It also includes provisions for the continuation of existing requirements and the possibility of repealing regulations through joint standards.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes regulatory oversight mechanisms for financial entities, requiring transparency and verification processes that impact data transfer and processing activities.

requirements.text: The Registrar Authority may require an applicant to furnish such additional information, or require such information to be verified, as the registrar may deem necessary

The Registrar Authority may take into consideration any other information regarding the applicant, derived from whatever source, including any other supervisory authority, if such information is disclosed to the applicant and the latter is given a reasonable opportunity to respond thereto
The Registrar Authority may prescribe any of the requirements referred to in subsection (1) in greater detail
Requirements prescribed under this section that are in force immediately before the commencement of this subsection continue to be in force
In respect of regulations prescribed in terms of subsection (1)(a), the Minister may repeal regulations, and new requirements may then be prescribed in joint standards or conduct standards
Paragraph (b) does not affect or limit the power of the Minister to prescribe or amend regulations in terms of subsection (1)(a)
Requirements prescribed in terms of subsection (1)(c) before the commencement of this subsection may be amended or repealed by conduct standards or joint standards

sections.source_quote: 36. Kuchitjiyelwa kwesigaba 55— (a) ngekuntjintjwa kwendzima (c) ngalendzima lelandzelako esigatjana (1): '‘(c) kukhombisa kutsi [letidzingo lekungito naletifanako letibhalwe ngunobhala] tidzingo lekungitona tifanele letincunywe kumtsetfo'

sections.heading: Kufakwa kwalesigaba lesilandzelako
sections.title: Licensing requirements for external trade repositories
sections.summary: This section outlines the licensing requirements for external trade repositories, including the need to publish application notices in national newspapers and on the Authority's website, the conditions for granting licenses, and the factors to be considered when assessing licenses. It also specifies the services and securities that may be provided by licensed repositories.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes licensing requirements for external trade repositories, impacting data transfers by requiring transparency and compliance with financial regulations for repositories handling sensitive financial data.

sections.summary: This section amends section 60 to specify that the Registrar Authority may cancel or suspend a license if the financial institution complies with directives, requests, conditions, or requirements of the Registrar Authority, or gives effect to decisions of the appeal board tribunal as per section 105.

sections.heading: The amendment of section 61—
sections.title: Amendment to section 61 for market infrastructure regulations
sections.summary: This section amends section 61 to prohibit market infrastructure from conducting business that would create or increase systemic risk. It also specifies that the Registrar Authority may make determinations on such business after consultation with the Prudential Authority and South African Reserve Bank, with requirements for draft determinations, submissions, and reasons for decisions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes restrictions on market infrastructure operations that could affect systemic risk, requiring detailed assessments of business activities and regulatory compliance before data transfers or processing.

requirements.text: Market infrastructure may not conduct business that creates or increases systemic risk
Registrar Authority may make determinations on business activities after consultation with Prudential Authority and South African Reserve Bank
Registrar Authority must provide reasons for determinations within 14 days

sections.source_quote: A market infrastructure may not conduct any additional business [which may introduce] if to do so would create or increase systemic risk.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers and systemic risk determinations

sections.heading: kuchitjiyelwa kwesigaba 63—
sections.title: Amendment to section 63 for entity conversion regulations
sections.summary: This section amends section 63 to specify that when an exchange, central securities depository, or independent clearing house converts to a company, certain rights, obligations, assets, liabilities, and agreements remain unaffected and binding on the new entity. This includes continuity of employee terms, assets, liabilities, and legal proceedings.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures continuity of data and financial obligations during entity conversions, critical for maintaining data transfer integrity and regulatory compliance across borders.

requirements.text: Continued corporate existence of exchange, central securities depository, or independent clearing house remains unaffected
Terms and conditions of employees remain unaffected
Assets and liabilities remain vested in the new entity
All agreements and transactions remain in force and effective

sections.source_quote: The continued corporate existence of the exchange, central securities depository, [or] independent clearing house or central counterparty from the date on which it was first licensed in terms of this Act is unaffected
sections.relevance_reason: Entity conversion continuity for financial data

sections.heading: The amendment of section 64 by the
sections.title: Amendment to section 64 for asset transfer regulations
sections.summary: This section amends section 64 to specify that assets and liabilities of amalgamating entities, including insurance, guarantees, and compensation funds, vest in the amalgamated entity or designated entity, ensuring continuity of obligations to clients.
sections.dtia_summary: This amendment ensures that data and financial obligations transfer seamlessly during entity amalgamations, critical for maintaining data transfer integrity and client protections.

requirements.text: Assets and liabilities of amalgamating entities vest in the amalgamated entity

sections.source_quote: all the assets and liabilities of the amalgamating entities (or in the case of a transfer of assets and liabilities, of the entity by which the transfer is effected), including any insurance, guarantee, compensation fund or other warranty owned or maintained by any of them to cover any liabilities of clearing members of independent clearing houses or central counterparties, authorised users or participants, as the case may be, to clients, vest in and become binding upon the amalgamated entity

sections.heading: The amendment of section 71—
sections.title: Amendment of section 71 for rule changes
sections.summary: This section amends section 71 to require that market infrastructure rules do not contradict regulations, conduct standards, prudential standards, or joint standards issued under this Act or the Financial Sector Regulation Act. It also outlines procedures for the Registrar Authority to amend rules, including publishing proposed amendments on the website and Gazette, and handling objections within 14 days. The amendments ensure that rule changes are transparent and subject to regulatory oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulates how market infrastructure rules are amended, requiring public notice and objection periods, which impacts data transfer assessments by ensuring transparency in rule changes affecting data handling.

requirements.text: Market infrastructure rules may not contradict regulations, conduct standards, prudential standards, or joint standards issued under this Act or the Financial Sector Regulation Act
Registrar Authority must publish proposed rule amendments on the website and Gazette
Registrar Authority must allow 14 days for objections to proposed rule amendments
Registrar Authority must publish amended rules and effective dates on the website and Gazette
Registrar Authority must provide reasons for rule amendments and explain the imperative in the Gazette and on the website

sections.source_quote: The [registrar] Authority may, after consultation with the Prudential Authority and the South African Reserve Bank, subject to this section, amend the rules or issue an interim rule.

sections.heading: Kuchitjiyelwa kwesigaba-74—
sections.title: Amendment of section 74 for conduct standards
sections.summary: This section amends section 74 to require that conduct standards are binding on authorized users, participants, clearing members, and their officers, employees, and clients. It ensures that conduct standards apply to all relevant parties, including those involved in market activities, to maintain ethical and compliant practices.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires conduct standards to be binding on market participants, impacting data transfer assessments by ensuring consistent application of conduct standards across data handling activities.

requirements.text: Conduct standards are binding on authorized users, participants, clearing members, and their officers, employees, and clients

sections.relevance_reason: Conduct standards binding on financial entities

sections.heading: Kuchitjiyelwa kwesigaba 77—
sections.title: Amendment of section 77 for insider definitions
sections.summary: This section amends section 77 to define 'inside information' as information that, if made public, would likely have a material effect on the price or value of securities listed on regulated markets or derivative instruments related to such securities. It also clarifies who qualifies as an 'insider' by including directors, employees, and shareholders of issuers of securities listed on regulated markets.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines inside information and insiders for regulatory purposes, impacting data transfer assessments by establishing criteria for insider information that may affect data handling and disclosure practices.

requirements.text: Inside information is information that, if made public, would likely have a material effect on the price or value of securities listed on regulated markets or derivative instruments related to such securities
Insiders include directors, employees, and shareholders of issuers of securities listed on regulated markets or derivative instruments related to such securities

sections.source_quote: if it were made public, would be likely to have a material effect on the price or value of any security listed on a regulated market or of any derivative instrument related to such a security
sections.relevance_reason: defines insider information and scope of insiders for financial data

sections.heading: Kuchitjiyelwa kwesigaba 78—
sections.title: Amendment of section 78 for insider offenses
sections.summary: This section amends section 78 to define insider offenses, including the requirement that insiders who disclose inside information for the purpose of proper employment functions are not guilty if they prove on a balance of probabilities that disclosure was necessary for their employment functions and unrelated to securities trading. It also addresses offenses related to encouraging or discouraging others from trading based on inside information.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines insider offenses and their defenses, impacting data transfer assessments by establishing legal frameworks for insider information handling and disclosure.

requirements.text: Insiders who disclose inside information for proper employment functions are not guilty if they prove on a balance of probabilities that disclosure was necessary for their employment functions and unrelated to securities trading
Insiders who encourage or cause others to trade based on inside information commit an offense

sections.source_quote: An insider who knows that he or she has inside information and who encourages or causes another person to deal or discourages or stops another person from dealing in the securities listed on a regulated market or in derivative instruments related to such securities, to which the inside information relates or which are likely to be affected by it, commits an offence.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: insider offenses not directly data protection

sections.heading: The amendment of section 82—
sections.title: Amendment of section 82 to specify claim submission deadlines
sections.summary: This section amends section 82 by substituting subsection (5) to require that claims must be submitted within 90 days from the date of publication of a notice in a national newspaper or on the Authority's website. It also modifies the notification process for claimants to prove their claims to the Authority.
sections.dtia_summary: This section sets a 90-day deadline for claim submissions and requires proof of claims, which impacts data handling and retention periods for financial claims.

requirements.text: Submit claims to the Authority within 90 days from the date of publication of a notice in one national newspaper or on the Authority's website
Prove to the reasonable satisfaction of the Authority that the claimant is affected by the dealings referred to in section 78(1) to (5)

sections.heading: The Authority may—
sections.title: Authority may establish conduct standards for market abuse
sections.summary: This section grants the Authority the power to establish conduct standards or regulator's directives for monitoring and identifying possible contraventions of the Financial Sector Regulation Act. It also allows the Authority to create conduct standards for the disclosure of inside information. The Authority must consult with relevant regulated markets before implementing these standards.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables the Authority to set standards for market abuse and inside information disclosure, which affects how financial data is handled and monitored across borders.

requirements.text: After consultation with relevant regulated markets, the Authority may make conduct standards for monitoring and identifying possible contraventions
The Authority may make conduct standards for the disclosure of inside information

sections.source_quote: 84. The Authority may— (a) after consultation with the relevant regulated markets in the Republic,— (i) make conduct standards, or (ii) give regulator's directives for the implementation of such systems as are necessary for the effective monitoring and identification of possible contraventions of this Chapter; and (b) make conduct standards for the disclosure of inside information.''
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for financial data standards

sections.heading: (1) (a) The Directorate established
sections.title: Directorate of Market Abuse established under Financial Sector Regulation Act
sections.summary: This section establishes the Directorate of Market Abuse as the continuation of the Insider Trading Directorate from previous legislation. It specifies the functions, powers, and duties of the Directorate, including conducting investigations into offences under sections 78, 80, and 81 of the Act and section 135(2) of the Financial Sector Regulation Act. The Directorate consists of members and alternate members appointed by the Authority.
sections.dtia_summary: This section creates a regulatory body for market abuse investigations, impacting how financial data is handled and monitored across borders.

requirements.text: The Directorate of Market Abuse continues to exist under the name Directorate of Market Abuse despite the repeal of previous Acts

sections.heading: The [directorate] Authority may
sections.title: Authority sharing financial information with regulators
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Regulation Authority may share information with specified entities including the Takeover Regulation Panel, South African Reserve Bank, Prudential Authority, Financial Intelligence Centre, National Treasury, and persons responsible for insider trading regulation. This applies to information related to matters dealt with under this Chapter. The sharing is permitted without specific conditions beyond the scope of the recipient entities.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows sharing of financial information with designated regulators for oversight purposes, relevant for cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions.

requirements.text: The [directorate] Authority may share information concerning any matter dealt with in terms of this Chapter

The South African Reserve Bank

The Independent Regulatory Board for Auditors constituted in terms of the Auditing Profession Act
A licensed exchange, a licensed central securities depository, or a licensed independent clearing house market infrastructure
The Financial Intelligence Centre established by the Financial Intelligence Centre Act
The National Treasury
The Minister
Persons inside the Republic or elsewhere responsible for regulating, investigating or prosecuting insider trading, prohibited trading practices and market abuses

sections.summary: This section amends section 90 to require regulated entities to maintain accounting records and prepare annual financial statements that conform with financial reporting standards prescribed under the Companies Act. sections.heading: (1) If the [registrar] Authority
sections.title: Investigation powers for financial misconduct
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Regulation Authority may investigate complaints or allegations of misconduct by securities service providers. The Authority may also require the respondent to appear before it at a specified time and place.

sections.heading: The amendment of section 105—
sections.title: Appeal mechanisms for financial decisions
sections.summary: This section amends section 105 to establish a grievance mechanism for persons aggrieved by decisions made by the Financial Sector Regulation Authority, exchanges, central securities depositories, or other entities under the Act. It covers appeals related to authorization, trading, and participation in securities markets.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a formal appeals process for financial decisions, relevant for data subject rights and dispute resolution in cross-border financial contexts.

requirements.text: A person aggrieved by a decision of the [registrar] Authority under a power conferred or duty imposed by this Act or the Financial Sector Regulation Act
An exchange to refuse an application by that person to be admitted as an authorised user
An exchange to withdraw the authorisation of an authorised user or to direct an authorised user to terminate the access to the exchange by an officer or employee of such authorised user
An exchange to defer, refuse or grant an application for the inclusion of securities in the list or to remove securities from the list or to suspend the trading in listed securities
A central securities depository to refuse an application by a person to be accepted as a participant

A central securities depository to terminate the participation of a participant or to direct a participant to terminate the access to the central securities depository by an officer or employee of a participant

sections.source_quote: 74. The amendment of section 105— (a) by the substitution for subsection (1) of the following subsection: '(1) A person aggrieved by a decision of— (a) the [registrar] Authority under a power conferred or a duty imposed upon the [registrar] Authority by or under this Act or the Financial Sector Regulation Act; (b) an exchange to refuse an application by that person to be admitted as an authorised user; (c) an exchange to withdraw the authorisation of an authorised user or to direct an authorised user to terminate the access to the exchange by an officer or employee of such authorised user; (d) an exchange to defer, refuse or grant an application for the inclusion of securities in the list or to remove securities from the list or to suspend the trading in listed securities; (e) a central securities depository to refuse an application by a person to be accepted as a participant; (f) a central securities depository to terminate the participation of a participant or to direct a participant to terminate the access to the central securities depository by an officer or employee of a participant;'
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: appeals mechanism for financial decisions

sections.heading: The amendment of section 108 by
sections.title: Amendment of fee determination authority in financial regulations
sections.summary: This section amends section 108 by substituting a subsection that allows the Authority to determine fees for matters under the Act. It specifies who must pay fees, the payment method, and interest for overdue fees. The Authority has discretion to set these fees within the framework of the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This amendment clarifies fee determination mechanisms but does not directly impact data transfer assessments as it pertains to fee structures rather than data flows.

requirements.text: The Authority may determine fees in respect of matters contemplated in this Act
The person by whom the fee must be paid is specified
The manner of payment of fees is specified
Interest payable in respect of overdue fees is specified

sections.source_quote: The Authority may determine fees in respect of matters contemplated in this Act and, in relation to those fees, the person by whom the fee must be paid, the manner of payment thereof and, where necessary, the interest payable in respect of overdue fees.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure amendment mechanics, fee determination

sections.heading: The amendment of section 110—
sections.title: Clearing house licensing requirements for central counterparties
sections.summary: This section amends section 110 by deleting subsection (5) and adding a new subsection (6). It requires clearing houses performing central counterparty functions to be licensed by the South African Reserve Bank and Prudential Authority by January 2022. The licensing process must be completed by December 31, 2021, with specific approval requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes licensing timelines and regulatory approvals for clearing houses, which may impact data transfer assessments if they handle financial data across borders.
sections.authority: South African Reserve Bank and Prudential Authority

requirements.text: Clearing houses performing central counterparty functions must comply with regulations or standards
Until 31 December 2021, clearing houses must be licensed as either associated or independent clearing houses and approved by the Authority, South African Reserve Bank, and Prudential Authority
As of 1 January 2022, clearing houses must be licensed as both independent and central counterparties

sections.source_quote: a clearing house performing the functions of a central counterparty must comply with any requirements imposed by regulations or standards, and must— until 31 December 2021, be licensed as either an associated clearing house or an independent clearing house, and be approved by the Authority, the South African Reserve Bank and the Prudential Authority
sections.relevance_reason: Licensing for financial data handling

sections.heading: The amendment of the arrangement
sections.title: Amendments to financial sector regulation arrangements
sections.summary: This section amends multiple sections of the Act by inserting new items, substituting headings, and modifying existing provisions. It introduces new items related to the relationship between the Act and Financial Sector Regulation Act, licensing criteria for external market infrastructures, and functions of licensed clearing houses and central counterparties.
sections.dtia_summary: This section restructures regulatory arrangements for financial entities, potentially affecting data transfer assessments through licensing requirements and regulatory oversight frameworks.

Authority is defined as the regulatory body
Criteria for recognition of external market infrastructures are established
Withdrawal of recognition of external market infrastructures is defined
Principles of cooperation for external market infrastructures are established
Licensing of clearing houses and central counterparties is defined
Requirements for applicants for clearing house and central counterparty licenses are specified

sections.heading: The amendment of section 1—
sections.title: Definition amendments for financial sector conduct authority
sections.summary: This section amends section 1 by inserting new definitions for 'Authority', 'conduct standard', 'Register', and 'Tribunal', while deleting definitions for 'deputy registrar', 'Financial Services Board Act', 'FSB official web site', 'prescribe', and 'registrar'. It clarifies the Authority as the Financial Sector Conduct Authority established under the Financial Sector Regulation Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines key regulatory entities and processes, which may indirectly affect data transfer assessments through regulatory oversight frameworks.

requirements.text: Authority means the Financial Sector Conduct Authority established in terms of section 56 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
Conduct standard has the same meaning as in section 1(1) of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
Register means the Financial Sector Information Register referred to in section 256 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
Tribunal means the Financial Services Tribunal established in terms of section 219 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act

sections.source_quote: ‘Authority' means the Financial Sector Conduct Authority established in terms of section 56 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
sections.relevance_reason: Definitions of key terms for financial data compliance

sections.heading: The insertion after section 1 of the
sections.title: Financial Sector Regulation Act alignment with other laws
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Financial Sector Regulation Act aligns with the Financial Sector Regulation Act. It requires that any provision of the Act that refers to a 'regulatory instrument' must be interpreted as a matter prescribed by the Authority where notice in the Gazette is specifically required. The section also specifies that references to inspections, prescribed fees, and reviews of decisions must be read as investigations, relevant fees determined under section 237, and reconsiderations by the Tribunal, respectively.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies how the Financial Sector Regulation Act interacts with other regulatory instruments and procedures, which is relevant for DTIAs when aligning data transfer mechanisms with existing regulatory frameworks.

requirements.text: A reference in this Act to an inspection must be read as a reference to an investigation in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
A reference in this Act to the Authority publishing information on a website must be read as a reference to publishing in the Register
The Authority may also publish information or documents on its website
A reference in this Act to a prescribed fee must be read as a reference to the relevant fee determined in terms of section 237 and Chapter 16 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act
A reference in this Act to a review of a decision of the Authority must be read as a reference to a reconsideration of the decision by the Tribunal

sections.source_quote: A reference in this Act to an inspection in terms of a provision of this Act must be read as a reference to an investigation in terms of the Financial Sector Regulation Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory alignment and interpretation of terms

sections.heading: Amendment of the arrangement of
sections.title: Amendment of the arrangement of sections
sections.summary: This section amends the arrangement of sections by inserting items 1A and 1B, which define the relationship between the Act and the Financial Sector Regulation Act and regulatory instruments. It specifies that any matter prescribed by the Authority requiring Gazette notice is considered a regulatory instrument for the purposes of the Financial Sector Regulation Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it establishes how regulatory instruments are defined and their relationship to the Financial Sector Regulation Act, impacting cross-border data transfers involving regulatory compliance.

Insert item 1B: Regulatory instruments
